- в¬ӣ **spВІ** вҶ’ trigonal planar, **120В°**, 3 Пғ + 1 delocalized ПҖ вҶ’ **graphene, graphite, CNT, fullerene**.
- в¬ӣ **sp** вҶ’ linear, **180В°**, 1 Пғ + 2 ПҖ вҶ’ **carbyne**; triple bond CвүЎC, length **1.20 Г…**.
- в¬ӣ **s-character** of sp^n = **1/(n+1) Г— 100%** вҶ’ 25%, 33.3%, 50% for spВі, spВІ, sp.
- в¬ӣ Higher s-character вҶ’ **shorter bond length** and **higher bond energy**.
- в¬ӣ **Bond lengths:** spВі = 1.54 Г… > spВІ = 1.42 Г… > sp = 1.20 Г….
- в¬ӣ **Bond energies:** CвҖ“C (spВі) вүҲ **347 kJ/mol** < C=C (spВІ) вүҲ **614 kJ/mol** < CвүЎC (sp) вүҲ **839 kJ/mol**.
- в¬ӣ **Pauling's formula:** $d(n) = d_1 - 0.71\log_{10}(n)$ reliably predicts CвҖ“C lengths for n = 1, 2, 3.
- в¬ӣ The **tetrahedral angle derivation** uses the **cube-corner geometry** leading to $\cos\theta = -\tfrac{1}{3}$.
- в¬ӣ **Graphene** = 2D monoatomic spВІ sheet; **CNT** = rolled graphene; **CвӮҶвӮҖ** = wrapped graphene (with pentagonal defects introducing curvature/strain).
- в¬ӣ In **fullerenes**, curvature causes pyramidalization: some **spВІ character is converted toward spВі**, lowering ПҖ-conjugation.
- в¬ӣ **Carbyne** exists as Оұ (cumulene, all double bonds) and ОІ (polyyne, alternating single/triple bonds).
- в¬ӣ All **spВІ carbons** exhibit **delocalized ПҖ systems** responsible for electrical conductivity, optical absorption, and aromatic chemistry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# рҹ”¬ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Carbon Allotropes in Nanotechnology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Carbon, in its various nano-allotropic forms, forms the cornerstone of modern nanotechnology. The three primary nano-carbon structures studied are **Fullerene (0D)**, **Carbon Nanotubes (1D)**, and **Graphene (2D)**. Together with bulk graphite and diamond, these represent the complete dimensional spectrum of carbon.

The element **Carbon (C, atomic number 6, atomic mass 12.011 g/mol)** exhibits extraordinary versatility due to its unique electron configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$, which allows for **sp, spВІ, and spВі hybridization**. This hybridization flexibility is the fundamental origin of all the diverse carbon nanostructures studied in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Concept:** Each carbon atom has 4 valence electrons. In nanostructures, the bonding configuration dictates the geometry: **spВІ hybridization** produces planar/curved sheets (graphene, fullerene, CNT walls), while **spВі hybridization** produces tetrahedral structures (diamond).

---

## 1.2 Fullerene (CвӮҶвӮҖ) вҖ” The "Buckyball"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Fullerene** is a closed-cage carbon molecule consisting of exactly **n carbon atoms** arranged in pentagonal and hexagonal rings, with the general formula $C_n$ where $n \geq 20$ (even number). The most stable and abundant fullerene is **CвӮҶвӮҖ** (Buckminsterfullerene), discovered by **Kroto, Curl, and Smalley in 1985** (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1996).

### Structural Description

CвӮҶвӮҖ is composed of **60 carbon atoms** arranged as a **truncated icosahedron**, identical in geometry to a soccer ball. The structure contains:
- **20 hexagonal faces** (6-membered rings)
- **12 pentagonal faces** (5-membered rings)
- **No two pentagons share an edge** (Isolated Pentagon Rule вҖ” IPR)

The carbon atoms are **spВІ hybridized**, but due to curvature, they possess a small degree of **spВі character (~10%)**, releasing ring strain.

### Intuitive Analogy рҹҸҶ

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a soccer ball. The black pentagons and white hexagons correspond to the 12 pentagonal and 20 hexagonal carbon rings in CвӮҶвӮҖ. Just as the soccer ball is a hollow, closed cage, so is CвӮҶвӮҖ вҖ” with a diameter of approximately **0.71 nm** and an internal cavity large enough to encapsulate small atoms (metallofullerene).

---

## 1.3 Carbon Nanotubes (CNT) вҖ” The "Cylindrical Buckyball"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Carbon Nanotube (CNT)** is a one-dimensional (1D) hollow cylindrical nanostructure formed by rolling a single (or multiple) sheet(s) of graphene into a seamless tube. Each CNT consists entirely of **spВІ hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in a hexagonal lattice.

### Classification

| Type | Abbreviation | Structure | Typical Diameter |
|------|--------------|-----------|------------------|
| Single-Walled CNT | SWCNT | Single graphene cylinder | **0.4 вҖ“ 2 nm** |
| Double-Walled CNT | DWCNT | Two concentric cylinders | **1 вҖ“ 3.5 nm** |
| Multi-Walled CNT | MWCNT | Multiple concentric cylinders | **2 вҖ“ 100 nm** |

### Intuitive Analogy рҹ“ң

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a single sheet of chicken wire (graphene) and roll it up like a straw. The resulting hollow tube is a **SWCNT**. Now, place one straw inside another slightly larger straw, and another inside that вҖ” you get an **MWCNT**.

### Chiral Configurations (Helicity)

The way graphene is rolled determines the chirality, defined by the chiral vector:

$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $n, m$ are integers and $\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2$ are graphene lattice vectors.

| Type | Condition | Geometry |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Armchair** | $n = m$ | High conductivity, **metallic** |
| **Zigzag** | $m = 0$ (or $n = 0$) | Either metallic or **semiconducting** |
| **Chiral** | $n \neq m \neq 0$ | Either metallic or **semiconducting** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A SWCNT is **metallic** if $(n - m)$ is a multiple of 3, otherwise it is **semiconducting**. Approximately **1/3** of all SWCNTs are metallic, and **2/3** are semiconducting.

---

## 1.4 Graphene вҖ” The "Wonder Material"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** **Graphene** is a two-dimensional (2D), single-atom-thick planar sheet of **spВІ hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in a hexagonal (honeycomb) lattice. It is the basic building block of all other graphitic carbon allotropes (rolled вҶ’ CNT, wrapped вҶ’ Fullerene, stacked вҶ’ Graphite). Isolated and characterized by **Geim and Novoselov in 2004** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 2010).

### Structural Description

- **Bond length:** CвҖ“C bond length = **0.142 nm (1.42 Г…)**
- **Lattice constant:** $a_0$ = **0.246 nm (2.46 Г…)**
- **Layer thickness:** Single atomic layer = **0.34 nm** (including van der Waals gap in graphite)

### Intuitive Analogy рҹҚҜ

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a single layer of atoms arranged like a honeycomb вҖ” each cell being a perfect hexagon with a carbon atom at every vertex. This is graphene вҖ” so thin (one atom thick) that it is essentially **2D material**. If graphite (pencil lead) is a stack of papers, graphene is a single sheet of that paper.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference: Dimensional Classification of Carbon Nanostructures**

| Property | Fullerene (CвӮҶвӮҖ) | CNT | Graphene |
|----------|----------------|-----|----------|
| **Dimensionality** | 0D (quantum dot-like) | 1D (wire-like) | 2D (sheet-like) |
| **Hybridization** | spВІ (with spВі character) | spВІ (pure) | spВІ (pure) |
| **Geometry** | Closed cage | Hollow cylinder | Planar sheet |
| **Typical Size** | Diameter ~0.71 nm | Length 1 nmвҖ“1 cm | Lateral size up to meters |
| **Discovery** | 1985 | 1991 (Iijima) | 2004 |
| **Building Block** | Wrapped graphene | Rolled graphene | Basic 2D unit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# вҡҷпёҸ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Synthesis Methods вҖ” Comprehensive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Fullerene Synthesis

#### Method 1: Arc Discharge Method (KrГӨtschmerвҖ“Huffman Method, 1990)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Vaporization of graphite electrodes in an inert gas atmosphere (Helium, low pressure ~100 torr) produces carbon soot containing fullerenes.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. Two high-purity graphite rods (anode and cathode) are placed inside a chamber filled with **Helium gas** at low pressure.
2. A high current (~100вҖ“200 A) at low voltage (~20вҖ“25 V) is passed between the electrodes, generating an **electric arc** (temperature ~3000вҖ“4000 В°C).
3. The anode is vaporized, producing a carbon plasma/plume.
4. Carbon atoms in the plasma collide and recondense on cooler surfaces, forming soot that contains CвӮҶвӮҖ and CвӮҮвӮҖ.
5. Fullerenes are extracted from the soot using a **non-polar organic solvent** (typically **toluene** or **benzene**).
6. Separation is achieved via **column chromatography** (typically using activated charcoal or silica gel).

**Yield:** ~10вҖ“15% of soot mass for CвӮҶвӮҖ + CвӮҮвӮҖ mixture.

#### Method 2: Laser Ablation / Laser Vaporization

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A high-power pulsed laser (Nd:YAG or COвӮӮ, ~1вҖ“10 J/cmВІ) is focused on a graphite target.
2. The target is placed inside a **quartz tube** heated to ~1200 В°C in an inert gas atmosphere (Ar/He at ~500 torr).
3. Carbon vapor is produced and swept downstream by the carrier gas, where it condenses on a cold collector.
4. The soot produced contains fullerenes (and SWCNTs, in case of catalyst addition).

**Advantage:** Higher purity; **Disadvantage:** Low yield and expensive.

#### Method 3: Combustion Method (Industrial Scale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** Combustion of hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene) in a low-pressure sooting flame produces fullerenes at industrial scale (~tons/year).

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A premixed hydrocarbon/oxygen flame is stabilized at low pressure (50вҖ“100 torr).
2. Conditions are tuned to favor fullerene formation (C/O ratio ~0.7вҖ“1.0).
3. Soot is collected and fullerenes extracted using solvents.

#### Method 4: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

1. Hydrocarbon gas (e.g., CHвӮ„, CвӮӮHвӮӮ) is introduced into a reactor at high temperature (500вҖ“1000 В°C).
2. Decomposition on a substrate produces carbon species that nucleate fullerene-like cages.
3. Less common than arc-discharge for bulk fullerene production.

---

### 2.1.2 Carbon Nanotube Synthesis

#### Method 1: Arc Discharge Method (Iijima, 1991)

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. Two graphite electrodes are placed in an inert gas (He/Ar, ~500 torr).
2. A catalyst (Fe, Co, Ni, or Y) is mixed into the anode.
3. An arc discharge (~100 A, 20 V) vaporizes the anode.
4. Carbon condenses on the cathode, forming MWCNTs (and SWCNTs if catalyst is used).
5. **Cathode deposit** contains MWCNTs; **web-like soot on chamber walls** contains SWCNTs.

**Yield:** Up to **30вҖ“60% MWCNTs** in cathode deposit.

#### Method 2: Laser Ablation

1. Laser pulses vaporize a graphite target doped with metal catalyst (Ni/Co, ~1вҖ“2 at.%).
2. The vapor is carried by flowing inert gas at ~1200 В°C.
3. SWCNTs form in the high-temperature zone.
4. **Produces high-quality SWCNTs** (~70вҖ“90% purity) with narrow diameter distribution.

#### Method 3: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) вҖ” Most Scalable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard:** CVD is the **dominant industrial method** for CNT production due to scalability and cost-effectiveness.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. **Substrate preparation:** Si or SiOвӮӮ wafer coated with catalyst nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni, ~1вҖ“100 nm) using sputtering or wet chemistry.
2. **Reactor setup:** Quartz tube inside a furnace heated to **600вҖ“900 В°C**.
3. **Carbon source:** Hydrocarbon gas (CвӮӮHвӮӮ, CHвӮ„, CвӮӮHвӮ„, or CO) introduced with carrier gas (HвӮӮ/NвӮӮ/Ar).
4. **Decomposition:** Hydrocarbon decomposes on catalyst nanoparticles via the **vaporвҖ“liquidвҖ“solid (VLS) mechanism**.
5. **Nucleation and growth:** Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle, become supersaturated, and precipitate as a tubular structure.

**Key Equations:**
$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{catalyst}} x\text{C} + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2 $$

**Tip-growth model:** Catalyst remains at the tip of the growing CNT (weak catalystвҖ“substrate interaction).
**Base-growth model:** Catalyst stays anchored to the substrate (strong interaction).

#### Method 4: High-Pressure Carbon Monoxide (HiPco) Process

1. CO at high pressure (30вҖ“50 atm) and temperature (900вҖ“1100 В°C) flows over Fe(CO)вӮ… catalyst in a reactor.
2. CO disproportionates: $2CO \rightarrow C + CO_2$.
3. Carbon atoms nucleate SWCNTs on iron nanoparticles.
4. Produces **high-quality SWCNTs** continuously.

---

### 2.1.3 Graphene Synthesis

#### Method 1: Mechanical Exfoliation (Scotch Tape Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Scotch Tape Method"** вҖ” Used by Geim and Novoselov (2004) to isolate graphene for the first time.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A piece of **highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG)** is repeatedly peeled using adhesive tape.
2. Repeated peeling thins down the graphite to single or few layers.
3. The thin flakes are transferred onto a **SiOвӮӮ/Si substrate**.
4. Single-layer graphene is identified by optical microscopy (due to interference contrast on SiOвӮӮ of specific thickness ~300 nm).

**Advantage:** Highest-quality monolayer graphene; **Disadvantage:** Not scalable.

#### Method 2: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

1. **Substrate:** Copper (Cu) or Nickel (Ni) foil.
2. **Annealing:** Substrate heated to ~1000 В°C in HвӮӮ/Ar to clean and recrystallize the surface.
3. **Carbon source:** Methane (CHвӮ„) introduced at low pressure or atmospheric pressure.
4. **Growth mechanism:**
   - On **Cu**: Surface-mediated (carbon dissolves minimally). Growth is **self-limiting** вҶ’ monolayer graphene.
   - On **Ni**: Precipitation-mediated (carbon dissolves and precipitates upon cooling). Layer thickness controlled by cooling rate.
5. **Transfer:** PMMA-assisted transfer to a target substrate.

**Equation:**
$$ \text{CH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu, 1000В°C}} \text{C (graphene)} + 2\text{H}_2 $$

#### Method 3: Epitaxial Growth on SiC

1. SiC substrate heated to **~1400вҖ“1600 В°C in ultra-high vacuum (UHV)**.
2. Si atoms sublimate preferentially, leaving behind a carbon-rich surface.
3. The remaining carbon rearranges into epitaxial graphene layers.
4. Produces wafer-scale graphene directly on a semiconducting substrate (electronics-compatible).

#### Method 4: Reduction of Graphene Oxide (RedGO)

1. **Oxidation:** Graphite is oxidized using **Hummers' method** (KMnOвӮ„ + HвӮӮSOвӮ„ + NaNOвӮғ) to form graphite oxide.
2. **Exfoliation:** Graphite oxide is exfoliated in water via ultrasonication to form **graphene oxide (GO)**.
3. **Reduction:** GO is reduced using chemical reductants (hydrazine, NaBHвӮ„) or thermal annealing (~1000 В°C) to form **reduced graphene oxide (rGO)**.

**Equation:**
$$ \text{GO} + \text{Reductant} \rightarrow \text{rGO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Caution:** rGO contains residual oxygen functional groups and defects; properties are inferior to pristine graphene.

#### Method 5: Liquid-Phase Exfoliation

1. Graphite is dispersed in a solvent (NMP, DMF) or aqueous surfactant solution.
2. Ultrasonication exfoliates the graphite into few-layer graphene flakes.
3. Centrifugation separates thin flakes from thicker ones.

---

## 2.2 Properties of Carbon Nanostructures

### 2.2.1 Properties of Fullerene (CвӮҶвӮҖ)

| Property | Value/Description |
|----------|------------------|
| **Molecular formula** | CвӮҶвӮҖ |
| **Molecular weight** | 720.66 g/mol |
| **Diameter** | ~0.71 nm |
| **Color (solution)** | Magenta/Purple (in toluene) |
| **Density** | 1.65 g/cmВі |
| **Melting point** | ~600 В°C (sublimes at ~400 В°C at low pressure) |
| **Band gap** | ~1.5вҖ“1.8 eV (semiconducting) |
| **Solubility** | Soluble in non-polar solvents (toluene, CSвӮӮ) |
| **Electron affinity** | ~2.65 eV (good electron acceptor) |

**Special Properties:**
- **Superconductivity:** Alkali-doped CвӮҶвӮҖ (e.g., KвӮғCвӮҶвӮҖ) becomes superconducting; **Tc вүҲ 18 K** (highest for molecular solids).
- **Electron acceptor:** Easily accepts electrons вҶ’ forms CвӮҶвӮҖвҒ», CвӮҶвӮҖВІвҒ», etc.
- **Endohedral fullerenes:** Metal atoms (La, Gd) can be trapped inside the cage (e.g., La@CвӮҶвӮҖ).

### 2.2.2 Properties of CNT

| Property | SWCNT | MWCNT |
|----------|-------|-------|
| **Young's modulus** | ~1 TPa | ~1вҖ“1.2 TPa |
| **Tensile strength** | 50вҖ“500 GPa | 10вҖ“60 GPa |
| **Density** | ~1.3вҖ“1.4 g/cmВі | ~1.7вҖ“2.1 g/cmВі |
| **Electrical conductivity** | Metallic or semiconducting | Generally metallic |
| **Current capacity** | ~10вҒ№ A/cmВІ (1000Г— Cu) | Lower |
| **Thermal conductivity** | ~3500 W/mВ·K (axial) | ~3000 W/mВ·K |
| **Aspect ratio** | 100вҖ“10,000 | 100вҖ“10,000 |
| **Specific surface area** | ~400вҖ“900 mВІ/g | ~200вҖ“400 mВІ/g |

**Unique Properties:**
- **Ballistic transport:** Electrons travel without scattering over micron-length scales.
- **Field emission:** Low turn-on voltage (~1вҖ“3 V/Ојm).
- **EMI shielding:** Superior due to high conductivity and aspect ratio.

### 2.2.3 Properties of Graphene

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thickness** | 0.34 nm (interlayer), single atomic layer ~0.142 nm |
| **Specific surface area** | **2630 mВІ/g** (highest for a monolayer material) |
| **Young's modulus** | ~1 TPa |
| **Intrinsic strength** | ~130 GPa |
| **Fracture strength** | ~42 N/m |
| **Electron mobility (suspended)** | ~200,000 cmВІ/VВ·s (room temperature) |
| **Sheet resistance** | ~31 О©/sq (theoretical minimum) |
| **Optical transmittance** | ~97.7% per layer (absorbs only 2.3% of visible light) |
| **Thermal conductivity (suspended)** | ~1500вҖ“5300 W/mВ·K |
| **Density** | ~2.267 g/cmВі |
| **Fermi velocity** | $v_F \approx 1 \times 10^6$ m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect:** Graphene exhibits the **quantum Hall effect at room temperature**, a property unique among 2D materials.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for KTU 2024 Exam вҖ” Must Memorize**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Chiral vector of CNT | $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$ | Defines tube geometry |
| 2 | Chiral angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m}\right)$ | Armchair: $\theta = 30В°$, Zigzag: $\theta = 0В°$ |
| 3 | CNT diameter | $d = \frac{a_0 \sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}$ | $a_0 = 0.246$ nm (graphene lattice constant) |
| 4 | Number of hexagons per unit cell | $N = \frac{2(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}$ | $d_R = \gcd(2n+m, 2m+n)$ |
| 5 | Metallicity condition | $(n - m) = 3k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ вҶ’ metallic |
| 6 | CвҖ“C bond length | $a_{CC} = 0.142$ nm | Same in graphene, CNT |
| 7 | Optical absorption of graphene | $A \approx \pi\alpha \approx 2.3\%$ | $\alpha$ = fine structure constant (1/137) |
| 8 | 2D electron density (graphene) | $n = \frac{\pi}{(\hbar v_F)^2} E_F^2$ | Dirac fermions |
| 9 | Aspect ratio of CNT | $AR = \frac{L}{d}$ | L = length, d = diameter |
| 10 | Fullerene cage atom count (CвӮҷ) | $n = 20 + 12k$, $k = 0,1,2,\ldots$ | Euler's formula |
| 11 | Pentagonal faces in fullerene | Always **12** | For any $C_n$ obeying IPR |
| 12 | Hexagonal faces in fullerene | $\frac{n - 20}{2}$ | For $C_n$ where $n \geq 24$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance for KTU:**

| Material | Key Applications |
|----------|------------------|
| **Fullerene** | Organic solar cells (PCBM acceptor), lubricants, MRI contrast agents (Gd@CвӮҶвӮҖ), HIV drug delivery (research), superconductors |
| **CNT** | Structural composites (sporting goods, aerospace), supercapacitors, field-emission displays, AFM tips, hydrogen storage, interconnects |
| **Graphene** | Flexible electronics, supercapacitors, sensors (single-molecule detection), batteries (Si anodes), transparent electrodes, biomedical |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# рҹ“җ Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Chiral Vector, Chiral Angle & Diameter of CNT

### Given:
A graphene sheet with lattice vectors $\vec{a}_1$ and $\vec{a}_2$, where:
$$\lvert \vec{a}_1 \rvert = \lvert \vec{a}_2 \rvert = a_0 = 0.246 \text{ nm}, \quad \angle(\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2) = 60В°$$

### Step 1: Express the chiral vector

The chiral vector is defined as:
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $n$ and $m$ are integers.

**Logic:** When graphene is rolled to form a CNT, the chiral vector $\vec{C}_h$ becomes the **circumference** of the tube. The two ends of $\vec{C}_h$ must coincide after rolling.

### Step 2: Compute the magnitude of the chiral vector

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert \vec{C}_h \rvert^2 &= (n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2)\cdot(n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2) \\
&= n^2 \lvert\vec{a}_1\rvert^2 + 2nm\,\vec{a}_1\cdot\vec{a}_2 + m^2 \lvert\vec{a}_2\rvert^2 \\
&= n^2 a_0^2 + 2nm\,a_0^2\cos(60В°) + m^2 a_0^2 \\
&= a_0^2 \left( n^2 + 2nm\cdot\frac{1}{2} + m^2 \right) \\
&= a_0^2 (n^2 + nm + m^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Using the dot product identity $\vec{a}_1\cdot\vec{a}_2 = a_0^2\cos(60В°) = a_0^2/2$.

### Step 3: Compute the diameter

Since $\vec{C}_h$ becomes the circumference of the rolled tube:
$$L = \pi d \quad\Rightarrow\quad d = \frac{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert}{\pi}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{d_{CNT} = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}}$$

### Step 4: Compute the chiral angle

The chiral angle $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{C}_h$ and $\vec{a}_1$. Using the dot product:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \frac{\vec{C}_h\cdot\vec{a}_1}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert\,\lvert\vec{a}_1\rvert} \\
&= \frac{(n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2)\cdot\vec{a}_1}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert\,a_0} \\
&= \frac{n a_0^2 + m\,a_0^2\cos(60В°)}{a_0^2 \sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \\
&= \frac{n + \frac{m}{2}}{\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \\
&= \frac{2n + m}{2\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\theta = \cos^{-1}\left( \frac{2n + m}{2\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \right)}$$

Equivalently:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m} \right)$$

### Verification with special cases:

| Case | $(n,m)$ | Predicted $\theta$ | Geometric Type |
|------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| Armchair | $(n,n)$ | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{3n}{2\sqrt{3}\,n}\right) = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 30В°$ | вң“ Armchair |
| Zigzag | $(n,0)$ | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2n}{2\sqrt{n^2}}\right) = \cos^{-1}(1) = 0В°$ | вң“ Zigzag |
| Chiral | $(n,m)$, $n \neq m$ | $0В° < \theta < 30В°$ | вң“ Chiral |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Metallicity Condition for SWCNT

### Quantum confinement of graphene band structure:

In graphene, the conduction and valence bands meet at the **K and K' points** of the Brillouin zone. The energy dispersion near these points is:
$$E(\vec{k}) = \pm \hbar v_F \lvert\vec{k} - \vec{K}\rvert$$

**Logic:** Graphene's charge carriers behave as massless **Dirac fermions** with linear dispersion (not parabolic like in conventional semiconductors).

### Quantization due to periodic boundary conditions:

When graphene is rolled into a CNT, the wavevector component along the circumference must satisfy:
$$\vec{k}\cdot\vec{C}_h = 2\pi q, \quad q \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This creates a set of **1D sub-bands** (cutting lines through the 2D graphene Brillouin zone). The number of sub-bands that pass through the K point determines if the tube is metallic.

### Condition for metallic tube:

The allowed k-points in the rolled structure are:
$$\vec{k}_\perp = \frac{2\pi q}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert}$$

The tube is **metallic** if one of these allowed lines passes through the K point. Mathematically:
$$\vec{K}\cdot\vec{C}_h = \frac{2\pi}{3}(2n + m) = 2\pi q_{\text{integer}}$$

This requires:
$$\frac{2n+m}{3} \in \mathbb{Z} \quad\Rightarrow\quad (2n + m) = 3k$$

Equivalent forms (using symmetry):
$$\boxed{(n - m) = 3k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}}$$

### Probability distribution:
- If $n, m$ are chosen randomly, $(n - m) \bmod 3$ is **equally likely** to be 0, 1, or 2.
- Therefore: **P(metallic) = 1/3, P(semiconducting) = 2/3**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Carbon Atoms in CNT Unit Cell

### Concept:
A unit cell of a CNT contains a finite number of hexagons, each contributing 2 carbon atoms.

### Step 1: Translational vector along the tube axis

The translational vector $\vec{T}$ is the shortest lattice vector perpendicular to $\vec{C}_h$ in the graphene lattice:
$$\vec{T} = \frac{2m + n}{d_R}\vec{a}_1 - \frac{2n + m}{d_R}\vec{a}_2$$

where $d_R = \gcd(2n+m, 2m+n)$.

### Step 2: Number of unit cells in the chiral vector

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{\lvert\vec{C}_h \times \vec{T}\rvert}{\lvert\vec{a}_1 \times \vec{a}_2\rvert} = \frac{2(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}$$

### Step 3: Total carbon atoms

Each unit cell has 2 atoms, and the unit cell contains $N_{\text{cells}}$ hexagons:
$$\boxed{N_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{4(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}}$$

### Example: Armchair (n,n) вҖ” e.g., (5,5) tube
- $d_R = \gcd(15, 15) = 15$
- $N_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{4(25 + 25 + 25)}{15} = \frac{300}{15} = 20$ atoms per unit cell вң“

---

## 3.5 Optical Absorption of Graphene вҖ” Analytical Derivation

### Fine structure constant and optical conductivity:

For pristine graphene, the optical conductivity is a constant:
$$\sigma(\omega) = \frac{\pi e^2}{2h}$$

for photon energies $\hbar\omega \gg k_B T$ and ignoring many-body effects.

### Optical transmittance:

For a single layer with thickness $t \ll \lambda$:
$$T = 1 - A = 1 - \frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0 c}$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi e^2}{2h}\cdot\frac{1}{2\epsilon_0 c} \\
&= \frac{e^2}{4\epsilon_0 h c} \\
&= \pi\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 \hbar c} = \frac{1}{137.036}$ is the fine structure constant.

$$\boxed{A \approx \pi\alpha \approx 2.3\%}$$

**Insight:** Each graphene layer absorbs exactly **2.3%** of incident visible light, regardless of wavelength. This is why graphite appears black (many layers absorb fully).

# рҹ“ҡ Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define fullerene. Explain the structure of CвӮҶвӮҖ with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fullerene** is a closed-cage carbon molecule consisting of $n$ carbon atoms arranged in pentagonal and hexagonal rings, following the general formula $C_n$ where $n \geq 20$ (even integer), satisfying the Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR).

**Structure of CвӮҶвӮҖ (Buckminsterfullerene):**
- CвӮҶвӮҖ is the most stable and abundant fullerene.
- Geometry: **Truncated icosahedron** (soccer-ball shape).
- Composed of **12 pentagonal faces** and **20 hexagonal faces**.
- Carbon atoms are **spВІ hybridized** with approximately **10% spВі character** due to curvature-induced strain.
- **Diameter вүҲ 0.71 nm**.
- No two pentagons share a common edge (IPR rule minimizes strain).
- CвҖ“C bond length: ~0.14 nm (shorter for hexagonвҖ“hexagon junction at 0.138 nm, longer for pentagonвҖ“hexagon at 0.145 nm).

**Euler's formula verification:** V вҲ’ E + F = 2 вҶ’ 60 вҲ’ 90 + 32 = 2 вң“

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is graphene? List any four unique properties of graphene.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphene** is a two-dimensional (2D), single-atom-thick, planar sheet of spВІ hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a perfect hexagonal (honeycomb) lattice. It is the basic structural unit of graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes. It was first isolated and characterized by **Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov in 2004** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 2010).

**Four Unique Properties:**
1. **Extraordinary electron mobility:** ~200,000 cmВІ/VВ·s at room temperature (suspended graphene) вҖ” far exceeding silicon (~1400 cmВІ/VВ·s).
2. **Optical transparency:** Absorbs only ~2.3% of visible light per layer (вүҲПҖОұ, where Оұ is fine structure constant).
3. **Record thermal conductivity:** ~1500вҖ“5300 W/mВ·K (suspended monolayer).
4. **Massless Dirac fermions:** Charge carriers behave as relativistic particles with zero effective mass, leading to anomalous quantum Hall effect observable at room temperature.
5. *(Bonus)* Highest specific surface area: **2630 mВІ/g**.
6. *(Bonus)* Young's modulus ~1 TPa, intrinsic strength ~130 GPa.

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### вқ“ Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a)** вҖ” 7 Marks
**Explain the KrГӨtschmerвҖ“Huffman method of fullerene synthesis in detail. What are the limitations of this method?**

#### **Part (b)** вҖ” 7 Marks
**Discuss the structure and properties of carbon nanotubes. Explain the different types of chirality with necessary equations.**

---

### рҹ“қ Model Solution вҖ” Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**KrГӨtschmerвҖ“Huffman Method (Arc Discharge Method for Fullerene Synthesis):**

**Principle:** Vaporization of graphite in an inert gas atmosphere using an electric arc produces carbon soot containing fullerenes.

**Apparatus Setup:**
- A **water-cooled stainless steel chamber** filled with **Helium gas** at low pressure (~100 torr).
- Two high-purity graphite rods positioned as electrodes:
  - **Anode** (vaporized): ~6 mm diameter
  - **Cathode**: ~9 mm diameter
  - Gap between electrodes: ~1вҖ“2 mm

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. The chamber is evacuated to ~10вҒ»вҒө torr and back-filled with He at ~100вҖ“200 torr.
2. A **high DC current (~100вҖ“200 A) at low voltage (~20вҖ“25 V)** is passed between the electrodes, creating an electric arc.
3. The arc generates temperatures of ~**3000вҖ“4000 В°C**, vaporizing the anode.
4. The vaporized carbon forms a **plasma plume** that condenses on cooler chamber surfaces as soot.
5. The soot is collected and dissolved in **non-polar solvents** (toluene, benzene, or CSвӮӮ).
6. **Column chromatography** (using activated charcoal or alumina) separates CвӮҶвӮҖ from higher fullerenes (CвӮҮвӮҖ, CвӮҮвӮҶ, CвӮҲвӮ„).
7. The pure CвӮҶвӮҖ solution is dried to obtain crystalline fullerene powder.

**Yield:** Typically 10вҖ“15% of soot mass (CвӮҶвӮҖ + CвӮҮвӮҖ).

**Limitations:**
- Low yield (most of the vaporized carbon forms amorphous soot, not fullerenes).
- High energy consumption.
- Requires continuous electrode replacement.
- Limited scalability for industrial production.
- Product mixture requires tedious chromatographic separation.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Structure of Carbon Nanotubes:**

A Carbon Nanotube is formed by rolling a graphene sheet into a seamless hollow cylinder. The structure can be of two types:
- **SWCNT:** Single graphene cylinder (diameter 0.4вҖ“2 nm)
- **MWCNT:** Multiple concentric graphene cylinders (diameter 2вҖ“100 nm), separated by ~0.34 nm (graphite interlayer distance)

**Properties of CNT:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Young's modulus | ~1 TPa |
| Tensile strength | 50вҖ“500 GPa (SWCNT) |
| Current density | ~10вҒ№ A/cmВІ (1000Г— Cu) |
| Thermal conductivity | ~3500 W/mВ·K (axial) |
| Field emission turn-on voltage | 1вҖ“3 V/Ојm |
| Aspect ratio | 100вҖ“10,000 |

**Chirality in SWCNT вҖ” Detailed Explanation:**

The geometry of a SWCNT is defined by the **chiral vector**:
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2$ are graphene lattice vectors ($|\vec{a}_1| = |\vec{a}_2| = a_0 = 0.246$ nm), and $n, m$ are integers.

**Three Types of Chirality:**

1. **Armchair (n = m):** Chiral angle $\theta = 30В°$. All armchair CNTs are **metallic**.
2. **Zigzag (m = 0):** Chiral angle $\theta = 0В°$. Metallic if $n$ is multiple of 3, else semiconducting.
3. **Chiral (n вү  m вү  0):** Chiral angle $0В° < \theta < 30В°$. Metallic if $(n-m)$ is multiple of 3.

**Diameter equation:**
$$d = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}$$

**Metallicity condition:**
$$\boxed{(n - m) = 3k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}}$$

If satisfied вҶ’ **Metallic**; else вҶ’ **Semiconducting**.

**Statistical distribution:** Approximately **1/3 metallic**, **2/3 semiconducting**.

---

### вқ“ Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a)** вҖ” 7 Marks
**Describe the CVD method for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes. Explain the vaporвҖ“liquidвҖ“solid (VLS) growth mechanism with a neat diagram.**

#### **Part (b)** вҖ” 7 Marks
**Compare the mechanical exfoliation and CVD methods of graphene synthesis. Discuss any three exceptional properties of graphene in detail.**

---

### рҹ“қ Model Solution вҖ” Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) of CNT вҖ” Step-by-Step:**

CVD is the **most widely used industrial method** for CNT synthesis due to its scalability and relatively low cost.

**Apparatus:**
- **Quartz tube reactor** placed inside a high-temperature furnace.
- **Mass flow controllers (MFC)** for precise gas delivery.
- **Substrate holder** in the central heating zone.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. **Substrate preparation:** Si, SiOвӮӮ, or quartz substrate is coated with catalyst nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni; size ~1вҖ“100 nm) using sputtering, e-beam evaporation, or wet chemical methods.
2. **Reactor setup:** Substrate is placed in the quartz tube.
3. **Purging:** Reactor is flushed with inert gas (Ar/NвӮӮ) to remove oxygen.
4. **Annealing:** Substrate is heated to **600вҖ“900 В°C** in HвӮӮ/Ar to reduce catalyst oxides and clean the surface.
5. **Carbon source introduction:** Hydrocarbon gas (CHвӮ„, CвӮӮHвӮӮ, CвӮӮHвӮ„, or CO) is introduced along with carrier gas (HвӮӮ/NвӮӮ).
6. **Decomposition:** Hydrocarbon decomposes on catalyst surface: $C_xH_y \rightarrow xC + (y/2)H_2$.
7. **Growth:** Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle, become supersaturated, and precipitate as a tubular structure.
8. **Cooling:** Reactor is cooled under inert gas to prevent oxidation.

**VLS Growth Mechanism:**

```
Catalyst Nanoparticle (Liquid Metal Droplet)
            вҶ“
Hydrocarbon Decomposition on Surface
            вҶ“
Carbon Atoms Diffuse into Catalyst
            вҶ“
Supersaturation
            вҶ“
Carbon Precipitates at Catalyst-Substrate Interface
            вҶ“
Lifts Catalyst вҶ’ Tip-Growth OR
Anchors Catalyst вҶ’ Base-Growth
            вҶ“
CNT Formation
```

- **Tip-growth:** Weak catalystвҖ“substrate interaction; catalyst lifts off as CNT grows.
- **Base-growth:** Strong catalystвҖ“substrate interaction; catalyst remains at base.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Comparison: Mechanical Exfoliation vs CVD for Graphene**

| Parameter | Mechanical Exfoliation | CVD |
|-----------|------------------------|-----|
| **Substrate** | SiOвӮӮ/Si (300 nm oxide) | Cu or Ni foil |
| **Method** | Scotch tape peeling | CHвӮ„ decomposition at 1000 В°C |
| **Quality** | Very high (pristine) | High |
| **Size** | Microns (small flakes) | Wafer-scale (continuous film) |
| **Cost** | Low (lab) | MediumвҖ“High |
| **Scalability** | Very poor | Excellent |
| **Layer control** | Single-layer identified by microscopy | Cu вҶ’ monolayer (self-limiting); Ni вҶ’ multilayer |
| **Transfer needed** | No | Yes (PMMA-assisted) |
| **Defects** | Very few | Grain boundaries, multilayer patches |
| **Application** | Fundamental research | Industrial electronics |

**Three Exceptional Properties of Graphene:**

**1. Exceptional Electron Mobility (вүҲ200,000 cmВІ/VВ·s):**
- Suspended graphene at room temperature shows electron mobility up to 200,000 cmВІ/VВ·s.
- ~140Г— higher than silicon (~1400 cmВІ/VВ·s).
- Charge carriers behave as **massless Dirac fermions** with Fermi velocity $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s.
- Energy-momentum relation: $E = \hbar v_F \lvert \vec{k} \rvert$ (linear, unlike parabolic).
- Enables **ballistic transport** over micron distances even at room temperature.

**2. Record Mechanical Strength (~130 GPa Intrinsic Strength):**
- Young's modulus ~1 TPa.
- Breaking strength ~42 N/m (force per unit length to break).
- ~200Г— stronger than structural steel by weight.
- Can be stretched up to 20вҖ“25% of its original length.
- Applications: flexible electronics, structural composites.

**3. Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect at Room Temperature:**
- The half-integer quantum Hall effect is observable in graphene at room temperature (200 K and even up to 300 K).
- Uniquely tied to its 2D Dirac-fermion nature.
- This has never been observed in any other material at room temperature.
- Opens applications in metrology and quantum computing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Property:** Optical transparency вҖ” graphene absorbs only **2.3% (вүҲ ПҖОұ)** of incident light per layer, independent of wavelength. This makes it ideal for transparent electrodes (replacing ITO).

## 5.3 вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Chirality equation confusion:** Many students write $d = a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}$ forgetting the $\pi$ in the denominator. The correct diameter is $\frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}}{\pi}$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Metallicity condition:** Students often write "metallic if $n = m$" вҖ” this is only true for armchair tubes. The general condition is $(n-m) = 3k$. **(Lose 1вҖ“2 marks)**
>
> 3. **SpВІ vs spВі hybridization in Fullerene:** Students incorrectly state CвӮҶвӮҖ is fully spВі hybridized. It is **spВІ with ~10% spВі character** due to curvature. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **CVD growth mechanism:** Writing "carbon deposits on substrate" without mentioning **dissolution in catalyst вҶ’ supersaturation вҶ’ precipitation** misses the key VLS mechanism. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Graphene unit:** Students sometimes write graphene is "1-atom thick = 0.34 nm." This is wrong вҖ” single atomic layer is ~**0.142 nm**; 0.34 nm is the **graphite interlayer distance**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Arc Discharge vs Laser Ablation:** Confusing which method produces MWCNTs (cathode deposit) vs SWCNTs (web soot / with catalyst). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 7. **Optical absorption:** Writing "graphene absorbs all light" вҖ” incorrect. It absorbs only **2.3% per layer**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 8. **IPR rule:** Students often forget to mention the **Isolated Pentagon Rule** (no two pentagons share an edge) when describing fullerene stability. **(Lose 0.5вҖ“1 mark)**
