## 5.4 рҹ“Ӣ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### рҹ”‘ High-Density Revision Checklist

#### Fullerene
- [ ] **CвӮҶвӮҖ** = Truncated icosahedron, **12 pentagons + 20 hexagons**, diameter **~0.71 nm**.
- [ ] Discovered by **Kroto, Curl, Smalley (1985)**; bulk synthesis by **KrГӨtschmerвҖ“Huffman (1990)**.
- [ ] **spВІ** hybridization with ~10% spВі character.
- [ ] Synthesis: **Arc discharge (He, 100 torr), Laser ablation, Combustion, CVD**.
- [ ] Extraction using **toluene**; purification by **column chromatography**.
- [ ] **Superconductivity** in alkali-doped CвӮҶвӮҖ (KвӮғCвӮҶвӮҖ, T_c вүҲ 18 K).
- [ ] **Electron affinity** ~2.65 eV вҖ” good electron acceptor (PCBM in organic solar cells).

#### Carbon Nanotubes
- [ ] **SWCNT**: single wall, d = 0.4вҖ“2 nm; **MWCNT**: multi wall, d = 2вҖ“100 nm.
- [ ] Discovered by **Iijima (1991)**.
- [ ] **Chiral vector**: $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$.
- [ ] **Diameter**: $d = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}}{\pi}$ where $a_0 = 0.246$ nm.
- [ ] **Chiral angle**: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m}\right)$; Armchair = 30В°, Zigzag = 0В°.
- [ ] **Metallic** if $(n-m)$ is multiple of 3; else **semiconducting**. (Ratio: 1/3 metallic, 2/3 semiconducting.)
- [ ] **Young's modulus ~1 TPa**; tensile strength 50вҖ“500 GPa; current density 10вҒ№ A/cmВІ.
- [ ] Synthesis: **Arc discharge, Laser ablation, CVD (most scalable), HiPco**.
- [ ] **VLS mechanism** in CVD; **Tip-growth** vs **Base-growth**.

#### Graphene
- [ ] **2D, single-atom-thick**, spВІ hexagonal lattice; discovered by **Geim & Novoselov (2004)**.
- [ ] **CвҖ“C bond** = 0.142 nm; **lattice constant** $a_0$ = 0.246 nm; **layer thickness** ~0.142 nm.
- [ ] Electron mobility ~200,000 cmВІ/VВ·s (suspended, RT).
- [ ] Young's modulus ~1 TPa; intrinsic strength ~130 GPa.
- [ ] Thermal conductivity ~1500вҖ“5300 W/mВ·K (suspended).
- [ ] **Optical absorption** = ПҖОұ вүҲ **2.3%** per layer.
- [ ] **Specific surface area** = **2630 mВІ/g**.
- [ ] Synthesis: **Mechanical exfoliation (best quality), CVD (scalable), Epitaxial on SiC, Reduction of GO (RedGO)**.
- [ ] **Dirac fermions** вҖ” linear dispersion; **anomalous QHE** at room temperature.

#### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- [ ] **All three nanostructures are derived from graphene** вҖ” the basic 2D building block.
- [ ] **spВІ hybridization** is the unifying bonding feature.
- [ ] **Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR)** explains fullerene stability.
- [ ] **Aspect ratio** of CNT = L/d (very high, up to 10вҒҙ).
- [ ] **Hummers' method** produces graphite oxide from graphite.
- [ ] **Fermi velocity** $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s in graphene (вүҲ c/300).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"FGC вҖ” Fundamental Graphene Connection"** вҖ” **F**ullerene = wrapped, **G**raphene = base, **C**NT = rolled. All are spВІ carbon with hexagonal lattice!

---

**END OF NOTES вҖ” Module 3: Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT**

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme вҖ” Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. Aligned with Course Outcomes (COs) and Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Dots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Dots (CDs / CQDs):** Carbon Dots are a novel class of **zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials** with sizes typically **below 10 nm**, composed primarily of an *spВІ/spВі* hybridised carbon core surrounded by surface functional groups (вҖ“OH, вҖ“COOH, вҖ“NHвӮӮ, C=O). They exhibit strong **size- and surface-state-dependent photoluminescence (PL)**, high aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity, excellent chemical inertness, and facile surface functionalizability вҖ” making them a versatile alternative to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdSe, CdTe) which contain toxic heavy metals.

According to the KTU 2024 PEMET868 syllabus framework, CDs are classified as a frontier "next-generation carbon nanomaterial" sitting alongside **fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)** in the broader carbon nano-family.

| Sub-Class | Distinguishing Feature |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)** | Clearly evident quantum confinement; quasi-spherical |
| **Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)** | Single/few-layer graphene fragments; anisotropic |
| **Carbon Nanodots (CNDs)** | Predominantly spВі carbon, amorphous, weak QCE |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Glowing Nano-Confetti"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine taking a tiny piece of burnt toast (carbon), grinding it down to a particle **80,000Г— smaller than the width of a human hair**, and then chemically treating its surface with вҖ“OH and вҖ“COOH "decorations." Under UV light, this nano-particle glows in brilliant blue, green, yellow, or red вҖ” much like **glow-in-the-dark stickers** used by children, but at a scale a billion times smaller. These luminous nano-confetti can be injected into cells to light them up, mixed into inks for anti-counterfeit labels, or used to detect toxic metal ions in drinking water by switching off their glow.

### 1.3 Historical Origin

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **2004** | Serendipitous discovery by **Xu et al.** during electrophoretic purification of SWCNTs |
| **2006** | Sun et al. coined the term *"Carbon Dots"* after laser ablation of carbon |
| **2010вҖ“2015** | Hydrothermal / microwave "green synthesis" routes emerged |
| **2015вҖ“Present** | Multi-disciplinary applications: bioimaging, LEDs, photocatalysis, theranostics |

### 1.4 Standard Nanoscale Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Critical Physical Constants for CDs:
> - **Typical diameter:** $\mathbf{1 - 10\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Lattice spacing (graphitic):** $\mathbf{0.18 - 0.24\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Specific Surface Area (BET):** $\mathbf{300 - 1500\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}}$
> - **Quantum Yield (QY):** $\mathbf{2\% - 90\%}$ (varies with synthesis)
> - **Largest emission window:** $\mathbf{400 - 800\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Density:** $\mathbf{\sim 1.5 - 2.0\ \text{g/cm}^3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Carbon Dot

Carbon dots possess a multi-tiered architecture that dictates their photophysical behaviour:

**Tier 1 вҖ” Carbon Core:**
- Crystalline (graphitic *spВІ*) **OR** amorphous (*spВі*) carbon framework
- Lattice fringes corresponding to the **(100) in-plane** and **(002) basal-plane** of graphite observed under HR-TEM
- Inter-layer spacing typically $\sim 0.34$ nm (similar to bulk graphite)

**Tier 2 вҖ” Surface Functional Groups:**
- Oxygen-containing: вҖ“OH, вҖ“C=O, вҖ“COOH, epoxy
- Nitrogen-containing (after doping): вҖ“NHвӮӮ, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N
- These groups act as **surface-state traps** that dominate PL emission

**Tier 3 вҖ” Passivation / Polymer Shell (optional):**
- PEG, PEI, PAMAM, silica вҖ” improves QY and biocompatibility

### 2.2 Photoluminescence Mechanism вҖ” "The Four-State Model"

The PL mechanism of CDs is still actively researched; however, the KTU-recommended model involves four coexisting pathways:

1. **Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE):** As particle size approaches the **exciton Bohr radius** of carbon, the bandgap widens, producing size-dependent emission. Smaller dots вҶ’ blue-shift.
2. **Surface State Emission:** Functional groups create mid-gap electronic states вҶ’ excitation-dependent emission (red-shift with increasing excitation wavelength).
3. **Molecular Fluorophore Emission:** Small organic fluorophores (e.g., citrazinic acid derivatives) embedded in/on the carbon matrix emit independently.
4. **Crosslink-Enhanced Emission (CEE):** Polymer-like crosslinked subdomains suppress non-radiative vibration, enhancing emission.

### 2.3 Synthesis Paradigm вҖ” Top-Down vs Bottom-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Mental Model:** "Top-Down" = **breaking** a bulk carbon source into nano-pieces. "Bottom-Up" = **building** nano-dots atom-by-atom from molecular precursors.

#### A. Top-Down (Physical Fragmentation)
- **Laser Ablation:** High-power pulsed laser strikes a carbon target submerged in solvent; CDs form in solution.
- **Arc Discharge:** By-product of CNT synthesis; CDs isolated chromatographically.
- **Electrochemical Oxidation:** Graphite rod anode oxidised in ionic liquid / aqueous electrolyte вҶ’ CDs peeled off into solution.
- **Chemical Oxidation:** Strong acids (HNOвӮғ, HвӮӮSOвӮ„) oxidise soot, activated carbon, or candle wax.

#### B. Bottom-Up (Molecular Assembly)
- **Hydrothermal / Solvothermal:** Carbon precursors (citric acid, glucose, urea) + water/organic solvent, heated in autoclave at 150вҖ“250 В°C for 3вҖ“12 h. *Most common lab method.*
- **Microwave-Assisted:** Rapid dielectric heating (seconds to minutes); energy-efficient.
- **Pyrolysis / Thermal Decomposition:** Carbonisation of organic molecules at 200вҖ“400 В°C in open air.
- **Plasma Treatment:** Atmospheric-pressure plasma jets convert precursors into CDs.

#### C. Green Synthesis (Bio-precursor route)
- CDs derived from **tea leaves, orange juice, egg-shell membrane, milk, honey, biomass waste** вҖ” eco-friendly, low-cost, highly favoured in KTU project evaluations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table for KTU ESE вҖ” these are the only equations repeatedly tested for the Carbon Dots topic.**

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Optical Bandgap from Absorption Onset | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{onset}} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}\ (\text{nm})}$ | Converts absorption edge to bandgap energy | eV |
| 2 | Quantum Yield (QY) вҖ” Relative Method | $\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{\eta_{CDs}^2}{\eta_{ref}^2}$ | Photonic emission efficiency vs reference dye (e.g., Quinine Sulphate) | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | Stokes Shift | $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_{abs} - \tilde{\nu}_{em} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{abs}} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{em}}$ | Energy loss between absorption and emission peak | cmвҒ»В№ |
| 4 | Quantum Confinement (Effective Mass Approx.) | $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$ | Bandgap widens as particle radius $R$ shrinks | eV |
| 5 | BET Surface Area | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{N_A\ \cdot\ V_m\ \cdot\ \sigma}{V_{molar}}$ | Quantifies accessible surface for catalysis/sensing | mВІ/g |
| 6 | Urbach Energy (Disorder) | $\alpha(h\nu) = \alpha_0\ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h\nu}{E_U}\right)$ | Measures structural disorder in carbon core | meV |
| 7 | Size from XRD Scherrer | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | Crystallite size from (002) peak broadening | nm |
| 8 | Stern-Volmer (Sensing) | $\dfrac{F_0}{F} = 1 + K_{SV}\ [Q]$ | Quenching constant for ion/molecule detection | MвҒ»В№ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **quantitative backbone** of any CD characterisation report. In research, they bridge synthesis (size) вҶ” optical property (bandgap) вҶ” application (sensitivity, catalytic rate, imaging contrast).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation вҖ” Quantum Yield by Relative Method

**Scenario (typical KTU numerical):** A carbon dot sample absorbs at 360 nm and emits at 450 nm. Using **Quinine Sulphate in 0.1 M HвӮӮSOвӮ„** as the reference ($\Phi_{ref} = 0.54$ at 360 nm), the following data is collected:

| Parameter | Quinine Sulphate (ref) | Carbon Dot Sample |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Integrated emission intensity $I$ | 50,000 | 78,000 |
| Absorbance at 360 nm $A$ | 0.10 | 0.08 |
| Solvent refractive index $\eta$ | 1.33 | 1.33 |

**Step 1 вҖ” Write the governing relative QY equation:**
$$\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{\eta_{CDs}^2}{\eta_{ref}^2}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute known values systematically:**

Because the solvent is identical (water), $\eta_{CDs}^2 / \eta_{ref}^2 = 1$. So the equation simplifies to:
$$\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}$$

$$\Phi_{CDs} = 0.54\ \cdot\ \dfrac{78000}{50000}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{0.10}{0.08}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Evaluate ratio 1 (Intensity ratio):**
$$\dfrac{78000}{50000} = 1.56$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Evaluate ratio 2 (Absorbance ratio):**
$$\dfrac{0.10}{0.08} = 1.25$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Multiply all three factors:**
$$\Phi_{CDs} = 0.54 \times 1.56 \times 1.25$$

$$0.54 \times 1.56 = 0.8424$$

$$0.8424 \times 1.25 = 1.053$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Interpret the result:**
$$\boxed{\Phi_{CDs} \approx 1.053 \rightarrow \text{physically clamped to } 1.00\ (\text{100\%})}$$

The "anomalous" $>1$ value arises because Quinine Sulphate is not a perfect reference; in practice, the student should report $\Phi_{CDs} \approx 100\%$ with a remark about reference calibration error. *(A real KTU answer expects the student to compute 1.05 and comment that it exceeds unity, indicating either experimental error or superior photon emission characteristics.)*

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation вҖ” Bandgap from Absorption Edge

**Given:** Absorption onset $\lambda_{onset} = 420$ nm (from UV-Vis Tauc plot).

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the bandgap formula:**
$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}\ (\text{nm})}\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute:**
$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{420} = 2.952\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Convert to Joules (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):**
$$E_g = 2.952 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$2.952 \times 1.602 = 4.729$$

$$E_g = 4.729 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} \approx \mathbf{4.73 \times 10^{-19}\ J}$$

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation вҖ” Quantum Confinement Radius Estimation

**Given:** Bulk graphite bandgap $E_g^{bulk} \approx 0$ eV (semi-metal), and a CD sample shows bandgap $E_g^{nano} = 2.5$ eV with reduced effective mass $\mu = 0.05\ m_e$ (where $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, Planck constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ JВ·s).

**Step 1 вҖ” Write the effective-mass equation:**
$$E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

Since $E_g^{bulk} \approx 0$:
$$E_g^{nano} = \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Solve for $R^2$:**
$$R^2 = \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu\ E_g^{nano}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Convert $E_g^{nano}$ to Joules:**
$$E_g^{nano} = 2.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 4.005 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Compute $\mu$:**
$$\mu = 0.05 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 4.555 \times 10^{-32}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Compute $h^2$:**
$$h^2 = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 = 4.390 \times 10^{-67}\ \text{J}^2\text{s}^2$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Substitute into $R^2$ equation:**
$$R^2 = \dfrac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{8 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-32} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19}}$$

Denominator: $8 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-32} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19} = 1.4595 \times 10^{-49}$

$$R^2 = \dfrac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{1.4595 \times 10^{-49}} = 3.008 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Take the square root:**
$$R = \sqrt{3.008 \times 10^{-18}} = 1.734 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \approx \mathbf{1.73\ nm}$$

**Diameter** $D = 2R \approx \mathbf{3.46\ nm}$ вҖ” consistent with typical CD size.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix вҖ” CDs vs Other Nanomaterials

| Property | Carbon Dots | CdSe Quantum Dots | Organic Dyes | Graphene Oxide |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Toxicity** | Very Low | High (CdВІвҒә) | LowвҖ“Moderate | Low |
| **Photostability** | High | High | Low (photobleach) | Moderate |
| **QY Range (%)** | 2вҖ“90 | 30вҖ“90 | 20вҖ“90 | 1вҖ“20 |
| **Size (nm)** | 1вҖ“10 | 2вҖ“15 | ~1 (molecular) | 100вҖ“1000 |
| **Cost (per g)** | Low | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Biocompatibility** | Excellent | PoorвҖ“Moderate | Moderate | Good |
| **Tunable Emission** | Yes (size/surface) | Yes (composition) | Limited | Limited |
| **Synthesis Complexity** | Simple | Complex (inert atm) | Simple | Simple |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-Away Matrix Insight:** Carbon Dots offer the **best balance of low toxicity, high photostability, low cost, and tunable emission** вҖ” this is precisely why KTU examiners emphasise CDs as a sustainable alternative to toxic QDs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram вҖ” Carbon Dot Architecture & Functional Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Carbon Dot Architecture] --> B[Core sp2 Carbon Framework]
    A --> C[Surface Functional Groups]
    A --> D[Optional Passivation Shell]

    B --> B1[Graphitic Crystalline sp2 Domains]
    B --> B2[Amorphous sp3 Carbon Regions]

    C --> C1[Oxygen Groups: OH COOH C=O Epoxy]
    C --> C2[Nitrogen Groups: NH2 Pyridinic Pyrrolic]
    C --> C3[Sulfur Groups: SO3H Thiol]

    D --> D1[PEG Polymer Coating]
    D --> D2[PEI Polymer Coating]
    D --> D3[Silica Shell SiO2]

    A --> E[Optical Properties Output]
    E --> E1[Photoluminescence 400 to 800 nm]
    E --> E2[Upconversion Emission]
    E --> E3[Chemiluminescence]
    E --> E4[Electrochemiluminescence]

    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    B1 --> E1
    B2 --> E2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram вҖ” Synthesis Routes to Carbon Dots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Synthesis Routes] --> TOP[Top Down Methods]
    SRC --> BOT[Bottom Up Methods]

    TOP --> T1[Laser Ablation]
    TOP --> T2[Arc Discharge]
    TOP --> T3[Electrochemical Oxidation]
    TOP --> T4[Chemical Acid Oxidation]

    BOT --> B1[Hydrothermal Solvothermal]
    BOT --> B2[Microwave Assisted]
    BOT --> B3[Pyrolysis Thermal]
    BOT --> B4[Plasma Treatment]
    BOT --> B5[Green Synthesis from Biomass]

    T1 --> CD[Carbon Dots]
    T2 --> CD
    T3 --> CD
    T4 --> CD
    B1 --> CD
    B2 --> CD
    B3 --> CD
    B4 --> CD
    B5 --> CD

    CD --> APP[Application Domain]
    APP --> AP1[Bioimaging]
    APP --> AP2[Chemical Sensing]
    APP --> AP3[Photocatalysis]
    APP --> AP4[LED Devices]
    APP --> AP5[Drug Delivery]
    APP --> AP6[Solar Cells]
    APP --> AP7[Anti Counterfeiting Inks]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram вҖ” Photoluminescence Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Photoluminescence Origins] --> P1[Quantum Confinement Effect]
    P --> P2[Surface State Emission]
    P --> P3[Molecular Fluorophore Emission]
    P --> P4[Crosslink Enhanced Emission CEE]

    P1 --> P1A[Smaller Particle вҶ’ Wider Bandgap вҶ’ Blue Shift]
    P2 --> P2A[Surface Trap States вҶ’ Excitation Dependent Emission]
    P3 --> P3A[Embedded Organic Chromophores Emit Independently]
    P4 --> P4A[Polymer Crosslinks Suppress Non Radiative Decay]

    P1A --> OUT[Tunable Multicolor Emission 400 to 800 nm]
    P2A --> OUT
    P3A --> OUT
    P4A --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Students:** The *architecture diagram (4.1)* is commonly asked as a 2-mark sketch question. Memorise the three-tier structure: **Core вҶ’ Surface Groups вҶ’ Shell**. The *synthesis diagram (4.2)* is the most frequently tested classification вҖ” recall at least **two top-down** and **three bottom-up** methods with one example precursor each.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
> **Define Carbon Dots. Mention any two distinguishing properties that make them superior to traditional semiconductor quantum dots.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, composed of an *spВІ/spВі* carbon core decorated with polar surface functional groups.

**Two distinguishing properties:**
1. **Low Toxicity & High Biocompatibility:** Unlike CdSe / CdTe quantum dots which release toxic CdВІвҒә ions, CDs are largely non-toxic and FDA-friendly, enabling safe in-vivo bioimaging.
2. **Cheap & Facile Synthesis:** CDs can be synthesised from inexpensive bio-precursors (glucose, citric acid, fruit extracts) via simple hydrothermal methods without inert atmosphere, in contrast to heavy-metal QDs requiring glove-box organometallic synthesis.

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
> **Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Principle | Bulk carbon fragmented into nano-pieces | Molecular precursors carbonised atom-by-atom |
| Energy Source | Mechanical / electrical / laser | Thermal / chemical |
| Size Control | Moderate (post-synthesis purification) | Good (precursor ratio tuning) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Example | Laser ablation of graphite in PEG solvent | Hydrothermal treatment of citric acid + ethylenediamine at 200 В°C for 5 h |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A.** `[KTU ESE Module 3 - Choice A]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **photoluminescence mechanisms** of carbon dots. Explain the role of **quantum confinement** and **surface states** with suitable equations.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **hydrothermal synthesis** of carbon dots from citric acid and ethylenediamine. Discuss how reaction parameters (temperature, time, precursor ratio) affect the optical properties.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Photoluminescence (PL) Mechanisms of Carbon Dots:**

**(i) Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE):**
When the CD particle radius approaches the exciton Bohr radius of carbon, electrons and holes are spatially confined, widening the effective bandgap.

$$E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

A smaller particle (smaller $R$) вҶ’ larger bandgap вҶ’ emission shifts to **shorter (blue) wavelengths**. Larger particles emit **red light**.

**(ii) Surface State Emission:**
Functional groups (вҖ“COOH, вҖ“C=O, вҖ“NHвӮӮ) introduce mid-gap electronic energy levels. Electrons excited to the conduction band relax non-radiatively into these surface traps, then emit at a lower energy.

$$\Delta\tilde{\nu}_{Stokes} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{abs}} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{em}}$$

The result is **excitation-wavelength-dependent emission** вҖ” a unique fingerprint of CDs.

**(iii) Molecular Fluorophore Emission:**
Small organic molecules (e.g., citrazinic acid, IPCA) formed *in situ* during carbonisation emit independently of the carbon core.

**(iv) Crosslink-Enhanced Emission (CEE):**
Sub-vibrational crosslinked polymer-like domains fix fluorophores in rigid environments, suppressing non-radiative decay and boosting QY.

**Conclusion:** The simultaneous operation of these mechanisms makes CDs **multicolour-tunable** without changing chemical composition.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Hydrothermal Synthesis of CDs from Citric Acid + Ethylenediamine (EDA):**

**Procedure:**
1. Dissolve **1 g citric acid** and **0.5 mL EDA** in 30 mL deionised water.
2. Transfer to **Teflon-lined autoclave** (50 mL capacity).
3. Heat at **200 В°C for 5 h** in an oven.
4. Cool naturally to room temperature.
5. Filter through **0.22 Ојm membrane** to remove large aggregates.
6. **Dialyse** (MWCO 500вҖ“1000 Da) against deionised water for 24 h.
7. Freeze-dry to obtain solid CD powder.

**Effect of Reaction Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on CD Properties |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Temperature (150вҖ“250 В°C) | Higher T вҶ’ larger carbonisation degree вҶ’ red-shift in emission |
| Time (3вҖ“12 h) | Longer time вҶ’ more surface groups вҶ’ higher QY (up to a saturation point) |
| Citric Acid : EDA Ratio | Higher amine ratio вҶ’ nitrogen-doping вҶ’ blue-shift, increased QY (>80%) |
| pH | Acidic conditions enhance вҖ“COOH passivation вҶ’ improved water solubility |

**Optical characterisation typically yields:** UV-Vis absorption at $\sim 340$ nm (nвҖ“ПҖ* of C=O), PL emission at $\sim 440$ nm (blue), QY $\sim 80\%$.

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU ESE Module 3 - Choice B]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **structural model** of carbon dots. Describe the role of **surface functional groups** in determining photoluminescence.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss any **four major applications** of carbon dots in bioimaging, sensing, photocatalysis, and LED fabrication, with mechanism for each.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Structural Model of Carbon Dots:**

CDs possess a **three-tier architecture:**

**Tier 1 вҖ” Carbon Core:**
Composed of *spВІ* graphitic nanocrystallites (with lattice spacing $\sim 0.34$ nm corresponding to (002) graphite plane) embedded in an *spВі* amorphous carbon matrix. HR-TEM images show clear lattice fringes in crystalline regions.

**Tier 2 вҖ” Surface Functional Groups:**
Pendant groups include:
- Oxygen: вҖ“OH, вҖ“COOH, C=O, epoxy (from incomplete carbonisation)
- Nitrogen: вҖ“NHвӮӮ, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N (after doping)
- Sulphur / Phosphorus (after doping)

**Tier 3 вҖ” Passivation Shell (optional):**
Polymer (PEG, PEI) or silica coating that enhances QY and colloidal stability.

**Role of Surface Groups in PL:**

1. They create **mid-gap surface states** that act as radiative recombination centres.
2. They determine the **HOMOвҖ“LUMO gap** of the trap states, controlling emission wavelength.
3. **Nitrogen doping** introduces pyrrolic-N which narrows the bandgap вҶ’ red-shift.
4. **Oxidation degree** correlates positively with QY up to an optimum.
5. Surface groups enable **solvatochromic behaviour** вҖ” emission shifts with solvent polarity.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Four Major Applications of Carbon Dots:**

**(i) Bioimaging:**
CDs are excited with UV/blue light and emit visible fluorescence. Their nano-size allows **cellular internalisation via endocytosis**. Surface functionalisation with folic acid / RGD peptides enables **targeted cancer-cell imaging**. Emission in the red/NIR region enables **deep-tissue in-vivo imaging** with reduced auto-fluorescence background.

**(ii) Chemical Sensing (Metal Ions / Biomolecules):**
CDs serve as fluorescent probes. In presence of analytes (e.g., FeВівҒә, HgВІвҒә, glucose), PL is **quenched** via static / dynamic quenching, photo-induced electron transfer (PET), or inner-filter effect. Sensitivity quantified by Stern-Volmer equation:

$$\dfrac{F_0}{F} = 1 + K_{SV}[Q]$$

High $K_{SV}$ values indicate strong sensor-analyte interaction.

**(iii) Photocatalysis:**
CDs act as visible-light photocatalysts due to their broad absorption and tuneable bandgap. They generate **electron-hole pairs** under illumination:
- Electrons reduce OвӮӮ вҶ’ вҖўOвӮӮвҒ» (superoxide)
- Holes oxidise HвӮӮO вҶ’ вҖўOH (hydroxyl radical)
These radicals degrade organic pollutants (rhodamine B, methylene blue) into COвӮӮ + HвӮӮO. CDs also catalyse **water splitting** (HвӮӮ evolution) and **COвӮӮ reduction**.

**(iv) LED Fabrication:**
CDs are dispersed in a polymer matrix (PMMA, PVK) and used as **colour-conversion layers** on blue LED chips. Blue light excites CDs вҶ’ down-converted white / yellow / red emission. Multi-colour CD-LEDs are fabricated by **size/doping-engineering** the CD emission peak.

---

### вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Carbon Dots Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing GQDs and CDs:** GQDs have anisotropic, layered graphene-like structure with lateral dimensions; CDs are quasi-spherical. Examiners deduct 1вҖ“2 marks for interchanging.
> 2. **Forgetting to cite specific examples:** Saying "carbon source" without naming citric acid, glucose, urea loses a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the equation in QCE questions:** Always write the **effective-mass formula** and substitute numerical values.
> 4. **Wrong units in QY calculation:** QY is **dimensionless** вҖ” never write "litres" or "molвҒ»В№".
> 5. **Missing QY reference dye:** When asked about QY measurement, you MUST name the reference (Quinine Sulphate, Rhodamine 6G).
> 6. **PL mechanism oversimplification:** Avoid saying "CDs glow because they're small." Always invoke the **4-state model**.
> 7. **Synthesis classification error:** Microwave, hydrothermal, and pyrolysis are **bottom-up**; do NOT classify them as top-down.
> 8. **Lack of diagrams:** In 14-mark answers, a labelled CD architecture diagram earns at least 1вҖ“2 marks even if text is incomplete.

---

### рҹ“Ң Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Carbon Dots:**
> - **Definition:** 0D fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, **< 10 nm**, with *spВІ/spВі* core + surface functional groups.
> - **Discovery:** Xu et al., **2004** (electrophoretic purification of SWCNTs).
> - **Types:** CQDs (spВІ, QCE), GQDs (graphene fragments), CNDs (spВі, amorphous).
> - **Top-Down methods:** Laser Ablation, Arc Discharge, Electrochemical Oxidation, Acid Oxidation.
> - **Bottom-Up methods:** Hydrothermal, Solvothermal, Microwave, Pyrolysis, Plasma, Green Synthesis.
> - **Most common lab route:** Hydrothermal (citric acid + ethylenediamine, 200 В°C, 5 h).
> - **PL mechanism:** Quantum Confinement + Surface States + Molecular Fluorophore + CEE.
> - **Key equations:** $E_g = 1240/\lambda_{onset}$ (eV); Effective-mass confinement; Stokes shift; Stern-Volmer quenching; Scherrer formula for size.
> - **QY reference dyes:** Quinine Sulphate (0.1 M HвӮӮSOвӮ„, $\Phi = 0.54$); Rhodamine 6G ($\Phi = 0.95$).
> - **Lattice spacing:** ~0.34 nm (002 graphite plane); size confirmed by HR-TEM & XRD.
> - **Advantages over CdSe QDs:** Non-toxic, cheap, eco-friendly, biocompatible, photostable.
> - **Major applications:** Bioimaging, Chemical Sensing, Photocatalysis, LEDs, Drug Delivery, Solar Cells, Anti-counterfeiting.
> - **Highest reported QY:** ~90% (N-doped, surface-passivated CDs).
> - **Typical UV-Vis peak:** ~340 nm (nвҖ“ПҖ* of C=O); emission blue ~440 nm (excitation-dependent).
> - **Sensing mechanism:** PL quenching (static / dynamic / PET / IFE) вҖ” quantified via Stern-Volmer plot.
> - **Photocatalysis active species:** вҖўOвӮӮвҒ» and вҖўOH radicals from photo-generated eвҒ»/hвҒә pairs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging 2D Materials: Transition Metal Dichalcogenides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging 2D Materials: Transition Metal Dichalcogenides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transition Metal Dichalcogenides (TMDs)** are a class of layered 2D materials with the general chemical formula **MXвӮӮ**, where **M** denotes a transition metal atom from groups 4вҖ“10 of the periodic table (e.g., Mo, W, Nb, Re, Ti, Hf) and **X** denotes a chalcogen atom (S, Se, or Te). In each monolayer, a single plane of metal atoms is covalently sandwiched between two planes of chalcogen atoms in an **XвҖ“MвҖ“X** configuration, while adjacent monolayers are held together by weak **van der Waals (vdW) forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TMDs are positioned as the **post-graphene** class of 2D materials because they offer a tunable, layer-dependent **direct bandgap** in the monolayer limit, which graphene fundamentally lacks. The reference materials are **MoSвӮӮ, WSвӮӮ, MoSeвӮӮ, WSeвӮӮ, and MoTeвӮӮ**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a TMD monolayer as a **club sandwich**:
- The two slices of bread on the outside are the **chalcogen (X) layers** вҖ” the sulfur/selenium/tellurium atoms.
- The meat/cheese filling in the middle is the **transition metal (M) layer**.
- The bread-meat-bread "sandwich" is held tightly by **covalent bonds**.
- Many such sandwiches can be stacked in a cafeteria counter, but they are merely leaning against each other, held by weak **van der Waals forces**, so you can peel them off one by one вҖ” this is **exfoliation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard in-plane lattice constant is **a вүҲ 0.31вҖ“0.33 nm** and the interlayer spacing is **c вүҲ 0.6вҖ“0.7 nm**, depending on the chalcogen atomic radius.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **In-plane lattice constant (a):** ~0.315 nm (MoSвӮӮ), ~0.315 nm (WSвӮӮ)
- **Bandgap range:** ~0.5 eV (bulk, indirect) вҶ’ ~1.8 eV (monolayer, direct)
- **SpinвҖ“orbit coupling (SOC) splitting at K-valley:** ~3 meV (MoSвӮӮ) up to **~450 meV (WSeвӮӮ)**
- **Exciton binding energy (monolayer):** ~0.5 eV (orders of magnitude higher than 3D semiconductors)
- **Carrier mobility (room temperature, monolayer):** ~10вҖ“500 cmВІ VвҒ»В№ sвҒ»В№
- **Effective electron mass (MoSвӮӮ monolayer):** ~0.35 mвӮҖ
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Structure and Polymorphism

TMDs exhibit three principal structural phases arising from the coordination geometry of the metal atom:

- **1T phase (octahedral):** DвӮғd point group; metallic character; thermodynamically stable for group-4 TMDs (TiSвӮӮ, ZrSвӮӮ).
- **2H phase (trigonal prismatic):** DвӮғh point group; semiconducting character; thermodynamically stable for group-6 TMDs (MoSвӮӮ, WSвӮӮ).
- **3R phase (rhombohedral stacking):** Same local coordination as 2H but with three layers per unit cell.

The structural phase can be **reversibly switched** between 2H (semiconducting) and 1T (metallic) via chemical doping, electron-beam irradiation, or alkali-metal intercalation вҖ” a property absent in graphene.

> [!NOTE]
> The notation "2H" means **2 layers per hexagonal unit cell**, and "1T" means **1 layer per tetragonal unit cell**.

### 2.2 Band Structure Evolution: Why the Bandgap Changes with Layers

The most celebrated KTU-style high-yield concept is the **direct-to-indirect bandgap crossover** when a bulk TMD is thinned to a monolayer:

- In **bulk 2H-MoSвӮӮ**, the valence band maximum (VBM) lies at the О“-point while the conduction band minimum (CBM) lies midway between О“ and K. This makes the bandgap **indirect (~1.2 eV)**.
- In **monolayer MoSвӮӮ**, quantum confinement along the out-of-plane direction lifts the energy of the О“-valley, pushing the VBM up to the K-point. The direct gap at K is **~1.8 eV**.
- The **K-valley direct gap is largely insensitive to layer number** because the K-point wavefunctions are localized within each layer and have minimal interlayer coupling.

### 2.3 Valley Physics and Spin-Valley Locking

Because the K and KвҖІ valleys in the hexagonal Brillouin zone are related by **time-reversal symmetry** but carry **opposite Berry curvature and opposite spin**, monolayer TMDs exhibit **spin-valley locking**. This is the foundation of **valleytronics** вҖ” using the valley pseudospin as an additional degree of freedom for information processing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_g^{\text{mono}} \approx 1.8 \text{ eV}$ (MoSвӮӮ) | Monolayer direct gap | eV |
| 2 | $E_g^{\text{bulk}} \approx 1.2 \text{ eV}$ (MoSвӮӮ) | Bulk indirect gap | eV |
| 3 | $\Delta_{\text{SOC}} = \lambda L_z$ | SpinвҖ“orbit splitting at K-valley | $\lambda \approx 0.04 \text{ eV (Mo)}, 0.43 \text{ eV (W)}$ |
| 4 | $E_b^{\text{exc}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 \epsilon^2} = \frac{4 \mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}$ | 2D exciton binding energy | 0.3вҖ“0.5 eV typical for ML |
| 5 | $a_{\text{exc}} = \frac{\hbar^2 \epsilon_r}{\mu e^2}$ | 2D exciton Bohr radius | ~1 nm in ML TMD |
| 6 | $m^* = \hbar^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial k^2} \right)^{-1}$ | Effective mass from EвҖ“k curve | kg or mвӮҖ units |
| 7 | $E(k) = E_0 \pm \alpha \vert \mathbf{k} \pm \mathbf{K} \vert$ | Linear dispersion near K/KвҖІ | $\alpha \approx 3 \text{ eVВ·Г…}$ (MoSвӮӮ) |
| 8 | $R_y = 13.6 \text{ eV}$ | Rydberg constant | Reference value |
| 9 | $\mu = \frac{m_e^* m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*}$ | Reduced mass of exciton | kg |
| 10 | $\Omega(\mathbf{k}) = i \langle \nabla_{\mathbf{k}} u \vert \times \vert \nabla_{\mathbf{k}} u \rangle$ | Berry curvature | Drives valley Hall effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** TMDs bridge the gap between zero-bandgap graphene (logic switches with low on/off ratio) and conventional 3D semiconductors. They are the **only known 2D family with intrinsic direct bandgaps in the visible-to-near-IR range**, enabling ultra-thin photodetectors, LEDs, and field-effect transistors (FETs).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Logic transistors (FETs):** Monolayer MoSвӮӮ FETs demonstrate on/off ratios > 10вҒё with sub-nm gate scaling.
- **Photodetectors / Solar cells:** Direct bandgap allows efficient light absorption in atomically thin layers.
- **Catalysis (HER):** Edge sites of 2H-MoSвӮӮ are highly active for the **Hydrogen Evolution Reaction**.
- **Valleytronic memory & logic:** Exploits spin-valley locking at K/KвҖІ.
- **Flexible electronics:** Mechanical flexibility arises from strong in-plane bonds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Monolayer Band Structure from a Three-Band Tight-Binding Model

We derive the low-energy band dispersion of a monolayer MXвӮӮ around the K-point using the **simplified three-orbital (d_zВІ, d_xy, d_xВІвҲ’yВІ) tight-binding model** for the transition-metal d-orbitals, as introduced in the foundational framework for MoSвӮӮ.

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the basis orbitals and their on-site energies.**
The three relevant transition-metal d-orbitals at each M site, expressed in the basis $\phi = \{ d_{z^2}, d_{xy}, d_{x^2-y^2} \}$, have on-site energies:
- $E_{z^2} = \epsilon_1$ (with $L_z = 0$ character)
- $E_{xy,x^2-y^2} = \epsilon_2$ (with $L_z = \pm 2$ character, Kramers split by SOC)

**Step 2 вҖ” Construct the nearest-neighbour hopping Hamiltonian.**
For a triangular lattice of metal atoms, the effective Hamiltonian at the K-point can be reduced to:

$$
H_{\text{TB}} = \begin{pmatrix} \epsilon_1 & 0 & t_0 \\ 0 & \epsilon_2 & t_1 \\ t_0 & t_1 & \epsilon_2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

where $t_0$ and $t_1$ are the hopping amplitudes between the $\ket{d_{z^2}}$вҖ“$\ket{d_{x^2-y^2}}$ and $\ket{d_{xy}}$вҖ“$\ket{d_{x^2-y^2}}$ orbitals, respectively. These terms reflect intra-layer dвҖ“d hopping.

**Step 3 вҖ” Diagonalize the 2Г—2 conduction/valence block.**
The $d_{z^2}$ orbital weakly couples to the conduction band minimum at K. The valence band maximum is dominated by the $d_{xy}$ and $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbitals, which mix to form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v^{\pm}(K) &= \frac{\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_2}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left( \frac{\epsilon_2 - \epsilon_2}{2} \right)^2 + t_1^2} \\
&= \epsilon_2 \pm \vert t_1 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Introduce spinвҖ“orbit coupling (intrinsic, atomic-like).**
At the K-point, time-reversal symmetry combined with broken inversion gives a large SOC that splits the valence band:

$$
\Delta E_v^{\text{SOC}} = 2 \lambda_{\text{atom}}
$$

where $\lambda_{\text{atom}}$ is the atomic SOC constant of the metal d-orbital. For W-based TMDs, $\lambda_{\text{atom}} \approx 0.21$ eV, leading to a splitting of $\sim 0.4$ eV, in agreement with ARPES measurements.

**Step 5 вҖ” Add the off-resonant coupling to obtain linear dispersion.**
The hybridization with remote $\ket{d_{z^2}}$ orbitals generates a momentum-dependent correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v(k) &= E_v^0 + \alpha \cdot \vert \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K} \vert \\
E_c(k) &= E_c^0 - \alpha' \cdot \vert \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

This linear, "Dirac-like" dispersion in the valence band is what makes monolayer TMDs respond to **circularly polarized light (valley-selective optical excitation)** вҖ” a result of the optical selection rule at the K and KвҖІ valleys.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Layer-Dependent Bandgap of 2H-MoSвӮӮ

| Number of Layers | Bandgap (eV) | Type | VBM Location | CBM Location |
|------------------|--------------|------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 (monolayer) | 1.80 вҖ“ 1.90 | Direct | K | K |
| 2 | 1.65 | Indirect | О“ | K (midpoint) |
| 3 | 1.35 | Indirect | О“ | K (midpoint) |
| 4 | 1.25 | Indirect | О“ | K (midpoint) |
| Bulk (вҲһ) | 1.20 | Indirect | О“ | midpoint О“вҖ“K |

> [!NOTE]
> The **K-point gap remains almost constant**, while the **О“-point energy rapidly rises** as the layer count decreases. This is the textbook origin of the direct-to-indirect crossover in KTU question banks.

### 3.3 Numerical Estimate: Exciton Binding Energy in Monolayer MoSвӮӮ

We use the 2D hydrogenic exciton model with effective masses $m_e^* \approx 0.35 \, m_0$, $m_h^* \approx 0.45 \, m_0$, and effective dielectric constant $\epsilon_r \approx 4.0$.

**Step 1 вҖ” Compute the reduced mass.**

$$
\mu = \frac{m_e^* \cdot m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*} = \frac{0.35 \times 0.45}{0.35 + 0.45} \, m_0 = \frac{0.1575}{0.80} \, m_0 \approx 0.197 \, m_0
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply the 2D exciton binding-energy formula.**

$$
E_b^{\text{exc}} = \frac{4 \, \mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}
$$

$$
E_b^{\text{exc}} = 4 \times 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{(4.0)^2} \text{ eV} = 0.788 \times \frac{13.6}{16} \text{ eV} \approx 0.67 \text{ eV}
$$

This matches the experimentally measured binding energy of ~0.5вҖ“0.7 eV for monolayer MoSвӮӮ, confirming the strong Coulomb interaction in the 2D limit.

**Step 3 вҖ” Compare to 3D bulk MoSвӮӮ.**

$$
E_b^{\text{3D}} = \frac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2} = 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{16} \text{ eV} \approx 0.17 \text{ eV}
$$

The factor-of-**4 enhancement** in the 2D case is a direct consequence of reduced dielectric screening in atomically thin layers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TMD Monolayer

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **atomic and electronic architecture of a 2H-MoSвӮӮ monolayer** (top view + cross-section + electronic consequence) without requiring a literal atomic drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transition Metal Atom M = Mo] --> B{Chalcogen Coordination}
    B --> C1[Top S Layer X1]
    B --> C2[Bottom S Layer X2]
    C1 --> D[Trigonal Prismatic X-M-X Sandwich]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[2H Phase D3h Symmetry]
    E --> F[In-plane lattice constant a ~ 0.315 nm]
    E --> G[Direct Bandgap ~ 1.8 eV at K Valley]
    G --> H[Valley Selective Optical Transitions]
    H --> I[Spin Valley Locking]
    I --> J[Engineering Applications: FETs LEDs Photodetectors Valleytronics]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Bandgap Evolution from Bulk to Monolayer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Bulk MoS2] -->|Layer 1: 1.20 eV indirect| N
    N1[3 Layers] -->|1.35 eV indirect| N1
    N2[2 Layers] -->|1.65 eV indirect| N2
    N3[Monolayer] -->|1.80 eV direct at K| N3
    N --> N1 --> N2 --> N3
    N3 --> KValley[Gamma VBM pushed up by quantum confinement]
    N3 --> KValley2[K valley direct gap stable due to localized d orbitals]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Synthesis Routes and Downstream Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Mechanical Exfoliation Scotch Tape Method] --> APP1[Lab Scale Prototypes]
    S2[Liquid Phase Exfoliation] --> APP2[Printed Electronics Inks]
    S3[Chemical Vapor Deposition CVD] --> APP3[Wafer Scale Device Fabrication]
    S4[Atomic Layer Deposition ALD] --> APP4[Conformal Thin Films on 3D Substrates]
    S5[Molecular Beam Epitaxy MBE] --> APP5[Ultra Clean Heterostructures]
    APP1 --> A1[Field Effect Transistors]
    APP2 --> A2[Flexible Sensors]
    APP3 --> A3[Photodetectors LEDs]
    APP4 --> A4[Energy Catalysis]
    APP5 --> A5[Superconductor Interfaces e.g. NbSe2]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Phase Engineering between 2H and 1T

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[2H Phase Trigonal Prismatic] -->|Thermodynamically Stable| P1
    P1 -->|Electron Beam Irradiation| P2[1T Phase Octahedral]
    P1 -->|Alkali Metal Intercalation Li K Na| P2
    P1 -->|Chemical Doping| P2
    P2 -->|Annealing 300 C| P1
    P1 --> Prop1[Semiconducting for Transistors]
    P2 --> Prop2[Metallic for Ohmic Contacts and Catalysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing "TMDs are similar to graphene" вҖ” partial credit loss. Always highlight the **direct bandgap** and **spin-valley locking** distinction.
> 2. Confusing 1T (metallic) with 2H (semiconducting) phases вҖ” both have the same MXвӮӮ stoichiometry but differ in coordination.
> 3. Stating the bandgap of monolayer MoSвӮӮ as "1.2 eV" вҖ” that is the *bulk* value. Use **~1.8 eV** for monolayer.
> 4. Forgetting to mention that the K-valley direct gap is **layer-independent** while the indirect gap is layer-dependent.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024]**
**Define Transition Metal Dichalcogenides (TMDs). Why are monolayer TMDs considered superior to graphene for digital logic applications?**

**Model Answer:**
TMDs are layered compounds with the general formula **MXвӮӮ**, where M is a transition metal (Mo, W, etc.) and X is a chalcogen (S, Se, Te). Each monolayer consists of an XвҖ“MвҖ“X covalently bonded sandwich, with adjacent layers held by van der Waals forces.

Monolayer TMDs (e.g., MoSвӮӮ) are superior to graphene for digital logic because:

1. Graphene is a **zero-bandgap semimetal**, so its transistors cannot be fully switched off (low on/off ratio ~10).
2. Monolayer MoSвӮӮ has a **direct bandgap of ~1.8 eV**, giving transistor on/off ratios > 10вҒё.
3. TMDs possess **spin-valley locking**, enabling valleytronic logic.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for graphene comparison)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Style]**
**Distinguish between the 1T and 2H phases of MoSвӮӮ with respect to crystal structure and electronic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | 1T Phase | 2H Phase |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Coordination | Octahedral | Trigonal prismatic |
| Point group | DвӮғd | DвӮғh |
| Layers/unit cell | 1 | 2 |
| Electronic character | Metallic | Semiconducting |
| Thermodynamic stability | Metastable | Stable (group-6 TMDs) |
| Typical use | Ohmic contacts, HER catalyst | FET channel, optoelectronics |

The 2H вҶ’ 1T phase transition can be induced by **Li-intercalation** or **electron-beam irradiation**, and is reversible by annealing.

*(3 marks: 1 for structure, 1 for property, 1 for example/transition trigger)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer either Question A or Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat band diagram, explain the **direct-to-indirect bandgap crossover** observed in 2H-MoSвӮӮ as the material is thinned from bulk to monolayer. Discuss the role of the О“ and K points and indicate the layer-dependent numerical values of the bandgap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the 2D exciton binding energy for monolayer MoSвӮӮ given $m_e^* = 0.35 \, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.45 \, m_0$, and $\epsilon_r = 4.0$. Compare it with the 3D bulk value and comment on the physical origin of the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bandgap crossover:**
- In **bulk 2H-MoSвӮӮ**, VBM lies at О“ (dominated by $d_{z^2}$ with strong interlayer coupling) and CBM lies at the midpoint between О“ and K. This makes the gap **indirect (1.2 eV)**.
- As layers are progressively removed, **interlayer coupling at О“ weakens** and the О“-point energy rises due to **quantum confinement** along the out-of-plane direction.
- The K-point states, being localized within each layer (mostly $d_{xy}$ and $d_{x^2-y^2}$), are **largely unaffected**.
- For the **monolayer**, the О“-point is pushed above the K-point, making the gap **direct (1.8 eV)** at K.
- Tabulated values:

| Layers | Gap (eV) | Type |
|--------|----------|------|
| Bulk | 1.20 | Indirect |
| 4 | 1.25 | Indirect |
| 2 | 1.65 | Indirect |
| 1 | 1.80 | Direct |

*(7 marks: 3 for mechanism, 2 for K vs О“ argument, 2 for table)*

**(b) Exciton binding energy derivation:**

Using $R_y = 13.6$ eV and the formula $E_b^{\text{2D}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \dfrac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}$:

$$
\mu = \frac{0.35 \times 0.45}{0.35 + 0.45} \, m_0 = 0.197 \, m_0
$$

$$
E_b^{\text{2D}} = 4 \times 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{(4.0)^2} = 0.67 \text{ eV}
$$

For 3D:

$$
E_b^{\text{3D}} = \frac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2} = 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{16} = 0.17 \text{ eV}
$$

The 2D value is **~4Г— larger** because the electric field lines of the Coulomb potential are confined in 2D, leading to unscreened electronвҖ“hole interaction. The reduced dielectric environment in a monolayer enhances binding.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical evaluation, 2 for physical explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** What is **valleytronics**? Explain the concept of **valley polarization** and **spin-valley locking** in monolayer MoSвӮӮ with reference to the K and KвҖІ points in the Brillouin zone. Discuss the optical selection rule for valley-selective excitation using circularly polarized light. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the major **synthesis techniques** for TMD monolayers and explain the **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** method in detail, including precursor chemistry, growth temperature, and substrate considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Valleytronics and spin-valley locking:**
- **Valleytronics** is the technology that exploits the **valley pseudospin** (the K and KвҖІ valleys of the Brillouin zone) as an information carrier, analogous to charge or spin.
- In monolayer MXвӮӮ, **inversion symmetry is broken**, but **time-reversal symmetry is preserved**. The K and KвҖІ valleys are inequivalent.
- Strong **spinвҖ“orbit coupling** from the heavy transition metal (e.g., Mo, W) splits the valence band at the two valleys. Crucially, the **spin index is locked to the valley index**: spin-up at K, spin-down at KвҖІ.
- **Optical selection rule:** Right-circularly polarized light ($\sigma^+$) excites carriers only at the **K valley**, while left-circularly polarized light ($\sigma^-$) excites carriers only at the **KвҖІ valley**.
- This enables optical generation of pure **valley-polarized populations**, observable via **circularly polarized photoluminescence (helicity-resolved PL)**.

*(7 marks: 2 valleytronics, 3 spin-valley, 2 optical rule)*

**(b) Synthesis routes & CVD details:**

Synthesis routes:

1. Mechanical exfoliation (Scotch tape)
2. Liquid-phase exfoliation
3. Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)
4. Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)
5. Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)
6. Sulfurization/Selenization of pre-deposited metal films

**CVD in detail:**
- **Precursors:** Molybdenum source: **MoOвӮғ** powder (heated to ~750 В°C); Chalcogen source: **S** powder (heated to ~200 В°C, vapor carried by Ar/NвӮӮ).
- **Substrate:** SiOвӮӮ/Si or sapphire, placed downstream in the cooler zone (~650вҖ“700 В°C).
- **Reaction:** MoOвӮғ is reduced by sulfur vapor to form volatile MoOxSy species, which diffuse to the substrate and nucleate 2D triangular MoSвӮӮ flakes.
- **Parameters:** Growth time (5вҖ“30 min), chamber pressure (low pressure ~10вҖ“100 Torr preferred for monolayer control), gas flow rate.
- **Result:** Wafer-scale continuous monolayer films achievable with grain sizes > 100 Ојm in optimized recipes.

*(7 marks: 2 list, 5 detailed CVD description)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **General formula:** MXвӮӮ (M = transition metal, X = S, Se, Te)
- **Structure:** XвҖ“MвҖ“X covalent sandwich; van der Waals interlayer coupling
- **Polymorphs:** **1T (metallic, octahedral)**, **2H (semiconducting, trigonal prismatic)**, 3R (rhombohedral)
- **Key materials:** MoSвӮӮ, WSвӮӮ, MoSeвӮӮ, WSeвӮӮ
- **Bandgap of monolayer MoSвӮӮ:** **~1.8 eV (direct)**
- **Bandgap of bulk MoSвӮӮ:** **~1.2 eV (indirect)**
- **Direct-to-indirect crossover** occurs at 2 вҶ’ 1 layer due to **О“-point quantum confinement**; **K-point gap is layer-independent**
- **Spin-valley locking:** Heavy metal SOC couples spin and valley pseudospin
- **Optical selection rule:** ПғвҒә excites K; ПғвҒ» excites KвҖІ
- **Exciton binding energy:** ~0.5вҖ“0.7 eV in monolayer (4Г— higher than bulk) due to reduced dielectric screening
- **Synthesis hierarchy:** Exfoliation (lab) вҶ’ CVD (wafer scale) вҶ’ MBE (cleanest heterostructures)
- **Applications:** FETs, photodetectors, HER catalysis, valleytronic memory, flexible electronics
- **Graphene vs TMD:** Graphene has **zero bandgap** вҶ’ poor switching; TMDs have **direct bandgap** вҶ’ excellent for digital logic
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hexagonal Boron Nitride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hexagonal Boron Nitride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN)** is a layered binary compound of boron (B) and nitrogen (N) in equal stoichiometric ratio (B:N = 1:1) that crystallizes in a hexagonal lattice isostructural to graphite. It is composed of atomically thin, two-dimensional (2D) sheets in which alternating B and N atoms are connected via strong, in-plane covalent **spВІ hybridized Пғ-bonds** and weak, out-of-plane **van der Waals (vdW) forces** between successive layers. Each BN sheet is commonly referred to as **"white graphene"** owing to its structural and morphological similarity to graphene combined with its characteristic wide-bandgap, optically transparent, and electrically insulating nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** h-BN belongs to the family of **2D layered materials (van der Waals solids)**. Its layered structure yields highly anisotropic properties вҖ” extremely strong in-plane covalent bonding (~510 kJ/mol for BвҖ“N) versus weak inter-layer vdW forces (~ 8вҖ“10 kJ/mol).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **h-BN as a "graphite clone"** with a key chemical twist:
- In **graphite**, every atom is carbon, so all CвҖ“C bonds are identical and the ПҖ-electrons (delocalized) make it an excellent in-plane conductor and a semi-metal.
- In **h-BN**, half the atoms are **boron (electron-deficient)** and half are **nitrogen (electron-rich)**. Because B and N have different electronegativities (Pauling scale: B вүҲ **2.04**, N вүҲ **3.04**), the ПҖ-cloud becomes **localized and polarized** between B and N pairs. The result: **ionic character within a covalent lattice**, which opens a large bandgap and makes h-BN an **electrical insulator** while keeping the mechanical and thermal properties of graphite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Picture:** Imagine a beehive (hexagonal tiles). Now paint half the tiles white (B) and half black (N) in a strict alternating pattern вҖ” that's the h-BN basal plane. Stack many such honeycombs on top of each other, held together not by glue but by a soft "velcro" of van der Waals forces, and you have h-BN.

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| In-plane lattice constant, *a* | **a = b = 0.2504 nm** |
| Out-of-plane lattice constant, *c* | **c = 0.6661 nm** |
| Inter-layer distance (van der Waals gap) | **d = 0.333 nm** |
| Theoretical in-plane stiffness | **~ 271 N/m** |
| Bandgap (theoretical, GW approximation) | **~ 5.95 eV** |
| Optical phonon energy (EвӮӮg mode) | **~ 1367 cmвҒ»В№** |
| Theoretical thermal conductivity (in-plane) | **~ 600 WВ·mвҒ»В№В·KвҒ»В№** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Atomic Structure & Bonding

**Layer Geometry (Basal Plane):**
- Each layer is a planar honeycomb of alternating **B and N atoms**.
- Bond length **BвҖ“N = 0.144 nm** (slightly longer than CвҖ“C in graphene = 0.142 nm).
- Bond angle **BвҖ“NвҖ“B = NвҖ“BвҖ“N = 120В°** (perfect hexagonal symmetry).

**Hybridization:**
- Each B atom: forms **3 Пғ-bonds** with three neighboring N atoms вҶ’ adopts **spВІ** hybridization.
- Each N atom: forms **3 Пғ-bonds** with three neighboring B atoms вҶ’ adopts **spВІ** hybridization.
- The remaining unhybridized **p_z orbitals** (one per atom, perpendicular to the plane) overlap laterally to form a **delocalized ПҖ-bond** вҖ” but unlike graphene, the ПҖ-electron cloud is localized because of the BвҖ“N electronegativity difference, producing a strong **ionic contribution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does h-BN have a wide bandgap?**
> Because of the partial ionic character (B donates electrons to N), the ПҖ and ПҖ\* bands are pushed apart by the on-site energy difference, opening a bandgap of **~ 5.5 вҖ“ 6.0 eV**. This is the **ionic bandgap mechanism**, in contrast to graphene's zero-gap semimetal behavior.

### 2.2 Layer Stacking (Polymorphs)

Three principal stacking orders exist (analogous to graphite's AA/AB stacking):

- **AA' stacking (Bernal, most stable):** B atoms of one layer sit directly above N atoms of the adjacent layer (B вҶ” N eclipsed). This is the **thermodynamically favored** stacking for bulk h-BN.
- **AB stacking:** B atoms of one layer above B atoms of the next; less common for h-BN.
- **AA stacking:** Like graphite's AA вҖ” energetically unfavorable for h-BN.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow (Bonding вҶ’ Properties)

1. **Strong spВІ Пғ-network in-plane** вҶ’ exceptionally high in-plane mechanical strength and Young's modulus.
2. **Polarized ПҖ-bond** вҶ’ wide bandgap вҶ’ electrical insulator.
3. **Weak inter-layer vdW forces** вҶ’ easy **mechanical exfoliation** (scotch-tape method) into **monolayer, bilayer, few-layer nanosheets**.
4. **Atomically flat, dangling-bond-free surface** вҶ’ ideal **dielectric substrate** for 2D electronics.
5. **High thermal conductivity + chemical inertness** вҶ’ suitable for **high-temperature, corrosive environments**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric ratio | B : N | **1 : 1** | Binary compound |
| 2 | Lattice constant (in-plane) | *a*, *b* | **0.2504 nm** | Hexagonal (P6вӮғ/mmc) |
| 3 | Lattice constant (out-of-plane) | *c* | **0.6661 nm** | Two layers per unit cell |
| 4 | BвҖ“N bond length | *d*_BвҖ“N | **0.144 nm** | Covalent Пғ-bond |
| 5 | Inter-layer spacing | *d*_vdW | **0.333 nm** | vdW gap |
| 6 | Energy bandgap | *E*_g | **~ 5.95 eV** | Insulator |
| 7 | In-plane stiffness | *C* | **~ 271 N/m** | 2D elastic constant |
| 8 | Bulk modulus | *B* | **~ 36.5 GPa** | Mechanical |
| 9 | Thermal conductivity | *Оә* | **~ 600 WВ·mвҒ»В№В·KвҒ»В№** | In-plane, monolayer theoretical |
| 10 | Refractive index (visible) | *n* | **~ 2.1** | Transparent |
| 11 | Dielectric constant (in-plane) | *Оө*_r | **~ 4 вҖ“ 5** | vs. SiOвӮӮ (~ 3.9) |
| 12 | Breakdown field | *E*_bd | **~ 1.2 V/nm** | Dielectric strength |
| 13 | Density | *ПҒ* | **~ 2.27 g/cmВі** | Bulk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Distinction:** When asked about lattice constants in KTU questions, remember *a* вүҲ **0.25 nm**, *c* вүҲ **0.67 nm** (вүҲ 2 Г— inter-layer spacing). Do **not** confuse with graphene's *a* вүҲ 0.246 nm.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of h-BN |
|---|---|
| **2D Electronics** | Atomically smooth gate dielectric for graphene/MoSвӮӮ FETs |
| **Optoelectronics** | Deep-UV emitter/detector (bandgap ~ 6 eV) |
| **Cosmetics** | "Boron nitride powder" вҖ” white, soft, lubricious filler |
| **High-Temp Lubricant** | Solid lubricant up to **~ 900 В°C** in air (vs. graphite ~ 400 В°C) |
| **Composites** | Thermal-management filler in polymers, ceramics |
| **Neutron Detection** | В№вҒ°B isotope has high neutron-capture cross-section |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BвҖ“N Bond Length from Lattice Geometry

For a **2D hexagonal honeycomb** with lattice constant *a* (distance between two nearest same-type atoms, e.g., B to next B), the BвҖ“N nearest-neighbor bond length **d_BN** relates to *a* by elementary geometry.

**Step 1 вҖ” Hexagonal geometry:** In a regular hexagon, the side length equals the circumradius. The nearest BвҖ“B distance equals the lattice constant *a* = 0.2504 nm. The center-to-vertex distance of the hexagonal BвӮғNвӮғ ring equals the same value *a*.

**Step 2 вҖ” Relate side to chord:** Consider an equilateral triangle formed by two N atoms and one B atom at the corner. The BвҖ“N bond length is the side of this triangle.

$$
d_{BN} \;=\; \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{BN} &= \frac{0.2504 \text{ nm}}{\sqrt{3}} \\
&= \frac{0.2504}{1.73205} \text{ nm} \\
&= 0.1446 \text{ nm} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.144 \text{ nm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Comparison with graphene:** Graphene's bond length *d*_CC = *a*_graphene / вҲҡ3 = 0.246 / 1.732 = **0.142 nm**. h-BN's bond is slightly longer because BвҖ“N is partially ionic and the covalent radius of B (~ 0.84 Г…) + N (~ 0.71 Г…) вүҲ 1.55 Г…, slightly greater than 2 Г— C (~ 0.77 Г…) = 1.54 Г….

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inter-Layer Spacing from c-Lattice Constant

The hexagonal unit cell of h-BN (space group P6вӮғ/mmc) contains **two layers** stacked in AA' fashion. Therefore:

$$
d_{vdW} \;=\; \frac{c}{2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{vdW} &= \frac{0.6661 \text{ nm}}{2} \\
&= \mathbf{0.3331 \text{ nm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value (~ 3.33 Г…) is virtually identical to graphite's inter-layer spacing (3.35 Г…), confirming the **van der Waals** nature of inter-layer coupling in both materials.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of Atoms per Unit Cell

A primitive hexagonal unit cell of h-BN with lattice constants (*a*, *a*, *c*) and angles (90В°, 90В°, 120В°) contains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Atoms/cell} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{from 8 corner atoms} \times 1/8} \;+\; \underbrace{1}_{\text{from 1 body-centre}} \\
&\quad \text{(NOT applicable for P6}_3\text{/mmc)} \\[4pt]
\text{Actual for } P6_3/mmc: \quad N_{atoms} &= \mathbf{4} \;=\; 2 \text{ B} + 2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each unit cell thus contains **2 B + 2 N = 4 atoms**, satisfying stoichiometric B:N = 1:1.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Properties vs. Graphene

| Property | h-BN | Graphene |
|---|---|---|
| Band structure | Insulator, *E*_g вүҲ **5.95 eV** | Zero-gap semi-metal |
| In-plane Пғ-bonding | spВІ (BвҖ“N, polarized) | spВІ (CвҖ“C, covalent) |
| Out-of-plane ПҖ-bonding | Localized, ionic | Delocalized, metallic |
| Electrical conductivity | Insulator | ~ 10вҒ¶ S/m |
| Thermal conductivity (mono) | ~ 600 W/mK | ~ 5300 W/mK |
| Optical absorption | Colorless / UV-active | ~ 2.3 % per layer |
| Chemical stability | Excellent (inert to acids) | Prone to oxidation > 300 В°C |
| Magnetic response | Non-magnetic | Non-magnetic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask "Why is h-BN an insulator while graphene is a conductor?" вҖ” answer: **partial ionic character of BвҖ“N bond + broken sublattice symmetry** (B вү  N) opens a large bandgap via the ionic contribution to the Hamiltonian.

---

### 3.5 Synthesis Methods вҖ” Comparison Matrix

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Exfoliation** | Scotch-tape peel from bulk crystal | Monolayer to few-layer flakes (lab scale) |
| **Liquid-Phase Exfoliation** | Sonication in solvents / surfactants | Nanosheets dispersed in solution |
| **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** | Decomposition of borazine (BвӮғNвӮғHвӮҶ) or B-trichloride + NHвӮғ on Cu/Ni at ~ 1000 В°C | Large-area continuous films |
| **PVD / Sputtering** | Ion-beam deposition of B in NвӮӮ atmosphere | Thin films |
| **BNNT вҶ’ BNNP (rolled sheets)** | Similar to CNT вҶ’ graphene route | Nanotubes & nanoparticles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Crystallographic & Functional Architecture of h-BN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Bulk h-BN Crystal]
    A1[Lattice: P6_3 over mmc]
    A2[Unit cell: a = 0.2504 nm, c = 0.6661 nm]
    A3[Atoms per cell: 2 B plus 2 N]
    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    B1[Basal plane: alternating B and N atoms in sp2 honeycomb]
    B2[In plane B-N covalent sigma bond length 0.144 nm]
    B3[Out of plane pi bond localized due to B N electronegativity difference]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    C1[Interlayer coupling: van der Waals gap 0.333 nm]
    C2[Stacking: AA prime Bernal]
    C3[Weak interlayer energy approx 8 to 10 kJ per mol]
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    D1[Wide bandgap insulator Eg approx 5.95 eV]
    D2[Atomically flat surface no dangling bonds]
    D3[High thermal conductivity approx 600 W per mK]
    D1 --> E1[Application: gate dielectric for 2D electronics]
    D2 --> E2[Application: atomically smooth substrate for graphene and MoS2]
    D3 --> E3[Application: thermal management in composites]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Synthesis-Route Decision Tree

```mermaid

    S0[Required h-BN Form]
    S1{Form Factor?}
    S0 --> S1
    S1 -->|Monolayer flakes| P1[Mechanical Exfoliation scotch tape]
    S1 -->|Dispersed nanosheets| P2[Liquid Phase Exfoliation sonication]
    S1 -->|Large area thin film| P3[Chemical Vapor Deposition on Cu or Ni]
    S1 -->|High purity thin film| P4[Sputtering of B in N2 atmosphere]
    P1 --> Q1[Lab scale research grade]
    P2 --> Q2[Composite and ink formulation]
```
