## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024]**
**Define photocatalysis. Explain with a neat sketch the mechanism of semiconductor photocatalysis involving electronвҖ“hole pair generation, migration, and surface redox reactions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a chemical reaction in the presence of a light-activated catalyst (photocatalyst) that is not consumed in the overall reaction. In semiconductor photocatalysis, when a semiconductor nanoparticle (e.g., TiOвӮӮ) absorbs a photon of energy hОҪ вүҘ E_g, an electron is excited from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving behind a hole in the VB. This electronвҖ“hole pair (eвҒ»/hвҒә) migrates to the surface where the electron reduces adsorbed OвӮӮ to В·OвӮӮвҒ» and the hole oxidizes HвӮӮO/OHвҒ» to В·OH. These reactive oxygen species mineralize organic pollutants to COвӮӮ and HвӮӮO. **(3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for mechanism explanation with labels)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]**
**Why are nanoparticles better photocatalysts than bulk semiconductors? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Higher surface-area-to-volume ratio** in nanoparticles provides a greater number of surface active sites per unit mass for adsorption and redox reactions. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Shorter migration distance** for photogenerated charge carriers from the bulk to the surface, which reduces the probability of electronвҖ“hole recombination. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Quantum confinement effects** in nanoparticles smaller than the exciton Bohr radius allow band gap tuning, enabling harvesting of visible light in materials like TiOвӮӮ that normally absorb only UV. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Photocatalytic Mechanism and Kinetics**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the mechanism of heterogeneous semiconductor photocatalysis with a band-structure diagram showing electronвҖ“hole pair generation, migration, and recombination. Compare the conduction and valence band edge positions of TiOвӮӮ, ZnO, and CdS with respect to water reduction and oxidation potentials.

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Heterogeneous Photocatalysis (5 Marks):**

The process involves five fundamental steps:

**Step 1 вҖ” Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy hОҪ вүҘ E_g is absorbed by the semiconductor nanoparticle, exciting a valence-band electron to the conduction band.

**Step 2 вҖ” ElectronвҖ“Hole Pair Generation:** This creates a conduction-band electron (eвҒ»_CB) and a valence-band hole (hвҒә_VB).

$$Semiconductor + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Charge Migration:** The eвҒ» and hвҒә diffuse to the catalyst surface. Nanoparticles offer shorter diffusion paths, reducing recombination.

**Step 4 вҖ” Surface Redox Reactions:** At the surface, electrons reduce electron acceptors (typically OвӮӮ) and holes oxidize electron donors (typically HвӮӮO or OHвҒ»).

$$O_2 + e^-_{CB} \rightarrow \cdot O_2^- \quad \text{(superoxide radical)}$$

$$H_2O + h^+}_{VB} \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+ \quad \text{(hydroxyl radical)}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Pollutant Degradation:** The ROS (В·OH, В·OвӮӮвҒ») attack organic pollutants, mineralizing them to COвӮӮ, HвӮӮO, and mineral acids.

**Step 6 вҖ” Recombination (Competing):** A fraction of eвҒ»/hвҒә pairs recombine, releasing energy as heat or light вҖ” this is the primary efficiency loss.

**Band-Edge Position Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Semiconductor | E_g (eV) | CB (V vs. NHE) | VB (V vs. NHE) | Activity |
|---------------|----------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| TiOвӮӮ (anatase) | 3.2 | вҲ’0.50 | +2.70 | UV-active, very stable |
| ZnO | 3.2 | вҲ’0.31 | +2.89 | UV-active, photocorrodes in acid |
| CdS | 2.4 | вҲ’0.60 | +1.80 | Visible-active, severe photocorrosion |

For overall water splitting: CB must lie above HвҒә/HвӮӮ (0 V) and VB must lie below OвӮӮ/HвӮӮO (+1.23 V). **All three materials satisfy thermodynamic requirements**, but only CdS operates under visible light.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a photocatalytic experiment, methylene blue dye (initial concentration 50 mg/L) is degraded using TiOвӮӮ nanoparticles (anatase, E_g = 3.2 eV) under UV irradiation. The concentration decreases to 5 mg/L in 90 minutes. Assuming pseudo-first-order kinetics:
**(i)** Calculate the rate constant k.
**(ii)** Determine the time required for 98% degradation.
**(iii)** Calculate the threshold wavelength of TiOвӮӮ and identify the spectral region.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rate Constant k (2 Marks):**

The pseudo-first-order integrated rate law is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = kt$$

Substituting CвӮҖ = 50 mg/L, C = 5 mg/L, t = 90 min:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{50}{5}\right) = k \times 90$$

$$\ln(10) = 2.3026 = 90k$$

$$k = \frac{2.3026}{90} = 0.02559 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Time for 98% Degradation (2 Marks):**

For 98% degradation, C = 0.02 Г— CвӮҖ:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.02}\right) = kt_{98}$$

$$\ln(50) = 3.9120 = 0.02559 \times t_{98}$$

$$t_{98} = \frac{3.9120}{0.02559} = 152.9 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ h } 33 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Threshold Wavelength and Spectral Region (3 Marks):**

Using the band gap formula:

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{1240}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{3.2} = 387.5 \text{ nm}$$

Since 387.5 nm < 400 nm, this falls in the **UV-A region (315вҖ“400 nm)** of the electromagnetic spectrum. Therefore, undoped anatase TiOвӮӮ can only be activated by UV light, which constitutes only ~4вҖ“5% of the solar spectrum вҖ” a key limitation driving research into visible-light-active photocatalysts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Nanostructuring, Applications, and Modifications**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss with examples how nanostructuring enhances the photocatalytic efficiency of TiOвӮӮ. Compare 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D TiOвӮӮ nanostructures in terms of surface area, charge transport, and light-harvesting ability.

**Model Solution:**

**Why Nanostructuring Enhances Photocatalysis (2 Marks):**

Bulk TiOвӮӮ (anatase) suffers from low surface area, rapid eвҒ»/hвҒә recombination, and can only absorb UV light. Nanostructuring addresses these limitations through:

1. **Increased surface-area-to-volume ratio** вҶ’ more active sites
2. **Reduced charge-carrier migration distance** вҶ’ less recombination
3. **Quantum confinement** вҶ’ tunable band gap
4. **Enhanced light scattering and absorption** вҶ’ better photon harvesting

**Comparison of TiOвӮӮ Nanostructures (5 Marks):**

| Dimensionality | Example | Surface Area | Charge Transport | Light Harvesting |
|----------------|---------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| **0D (Quantum Dots)** | TiOвӮӮ QDs (~3вҖ“5 nm) | Very high | Poor (many grain boundaries) | Excellent (tunable band gap via QC) |
| **1D (Nanorods/Nanotubes)** | TiOвӮӮ nanotubes (NTs) | High | **Excellent** (direct pathway along axis) | Good (light trapping in tubular geometry) |
| **2D (Nanosheets)** | Exfoliated TiOвӮӮ nanosheets with exposed {001} facets | Very high (two exposed basal planes) | Moderate (in-plane transport) | Excellent (thinness ensures short migration distance) |
| **3D (Mesoporous/Forest)** | TiOвӮӮ nanoflowers, inverse opals | High with interconnected pores | Moderate (network junctions) | Excellent (multiple scattering in hierarchical pores) |

**Best Performer:** 1D TiOвӮӮ nanotubes are generally considered the most balanced architecture for photocatalysis because they provide direct, low-resistance electron transport along the tube axis while maintaining a high surface area and a short radial diffusion distance (~10вҖ“15 nm wall thickness) for holes to reach the surface. They are widely used in **dye-sensitized solar cells and water-splitting photoanodes**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe any three major applications of semiconductor photocatalysis in environmental remediation and energy. For each application, name the target reaction and explain the role of the photocatalyst.

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1 вҖ” Water Treatment via Dye Degradation (2.5 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Mineralization of organic dyes (methylene blue, rhodamine B, methyl orange) released from textile industries.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** TiOвӮӮ (Degussa P25) nanoparticles, when illuminated with UV light, generate В·OH radicals that non-selectively oxidize the chromophoric groups of dye molecules, breaking them into COвӮӮ, HвӮӮO, NOвӮғвҒ», and SOвӮ„ВІвҒ».

$$\text{Dye} + \cdot OH \rightarrow \text{intermediates} \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{mineral acids}$$

**Significance:** This is a "green" alternative to chlorine-based water treatment, which generates carcinogenic chlorinated byproducts.

**Application 2 вҖ” Photocatalytic Hydrogen Generation (2.5 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Overall water splitting into HвӮӮ and OвӮӮ using sunlight.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** Modified TiOвӮӮ (e.g., Pt-loaded, N-doped) or visible-light photocatalysts (CdS, g-CвӮғNвӮ„) absorb sunlight and use the photogenerated electrons to reduce water at the CB while holes oxidize water at the VB.

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{photocatalyst}, h\nu} 2H_2 + O_2$$

**Significance:** Produces **green hydrogen** вҖ” a zero-carbon fuel. Coupled with solar energy, this offers a sustainable alternative to steam-methane reforming (which emits 10 tons of COвӮӮ per ton of HвӮӮ produced).

**Application 3 вҖ” Self-Cleaning and Antibacterial Surfaces (2 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Decomposition of organic dirt, oils, and bacterial cell walls on coated surfaces.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** A thin TiOвӮӮ film (~50вҖ“100 nm) is coated on glass, tiles, or medical devices. Under sunlight or indoor UV, the film generates ROS that oxidize organic contaminants and rupture bacterial membranes (E. coli, S. aureus). The surface becomes hydrophilic (water contact angle вҶ’ 0В°) via photo-induced surface restructuring, allowing rainwater to sheet off and wash away loosened dirt.

**Significance:** Reduces maintenance cost of buildings, hospital-acquired infections, and airborne disease transmission вҖ” a major post-COVID application area.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Photocatalysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Band gap unit confusion:** Students frequently mix eV and J. KTU examiners award **zero marks** if units are inconsistent. Always state "E_g = 3.2 eV = 5.13 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ№ J" explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing pseudo-first-order with zero-order kinetics:** Photocatalytic dye degradation is **first-order in dye concentration at low concentrations (<10вҒ»Ві M)**. Do not write the zero-order equation [C = CвӮҖ вҲ’ kt] unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in hydrogen quantum yield:** Producing 1 mole of HвӮӮ requires **2 electrons вҶ’ 2 photons**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missed.
> 4. **Misidentifying spectral regions:** UV-A: 315вҖ“400 nm, UV-B: 280вҖ“315 nm, UV-C: 100вҖ“280 nm, Visible: 400вҖ“700 nm. TiOвӮӮ (E_g вүҲ 3.2 eV) is UV-A active, NOT visible.
> 5. **Skipping the band-edge diagram:** Even a rough sketch of VB and CB positions with the HвҒә/HвӮӮ and OвӮӮ/HвӮӮO redox levels fetches **2 full marks** in 14-mark questions. KTU examiners have a strong bias for labelled diagrams.
> 6. **Writing "TiOвӮӮ is visible-light active":** Pure anatase TiOвӮӮ is a **UV-only** photocatalyst. Only doped or heterojunction TiOвӮӮ extends into the visible region. This single factual error can cost 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist вҖ” Photocatalysis**
>
> вң… **Core Definition:** Photocatalysis = light + catalyst (not consumed) accelerates a reaction.
>
> вң… **Active Materials:** TiOвӮӮ (anatase), ZnO, FeвӮӮOвӮғ, CdS, g-CвӮғNвӮ„, BiVOвӮ„, WOвӮғ.
>
> вң… **Band Gap Threshold Condition:** Photon energy hОҪ вүҘ E_g for activation.
>
> вң… **Master Formula:** О»_threshold (nm) = 1240 / E_g (eV).
>
> вң… **Six-Step Mechanism:** Absorption вҶ’ eвҒ»/hвҒә generation вҶ’ migration вҶ’ surface adsorption вҶ’ redox reactions вҶ’ recombination (loss).
>
> вң… **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS):** В·OH, В·OвӮӮвҒ», HвӮӮOвӮӮ вҖ” these are the actual chemical agents.
>
> вң… **Kinetics:** Pseudo-first-order: ln(CвӮҖ/C) = kt (most common in KTU problems).
>
> вң… **LangmuirвҖ“Hinshelwood:** r = kKC / (1 + KC); used for high-concentration or surface-saturated reactions.
>
> вң… **Why Nano:** Higher surface area + shorter charge migration + quantum confinement = superior photocatalysis.
>
> вң… **Band Edge Requirements:** For water splitting, CB < 0 V and VB > +1.23 V vs. NHE (at pH 0).
>
> вң… **Enhancement Strategies:** Doping, heterojunctions, dye sensitization, plasmonic coupling, nanostructuring, cocatalyst loading (Pt, Au).
>
> вң… **Major Applications:** Dye degradation, water splitting (HвӮӮ), COвӮӮ reduction, self-cleaning glass, antibacterial coatings, indoor air purification.
>
> вң… **Key Limitation:** TiOвӮӮ absorbs only ~4вҖ“5% of solar spectrum вҶ’ drives research into visible-light-active materials.
>
> вң… **Photo-corrosion:** A serious issue for CdS, ZnO вҖ” electrons/holes oxidize the catalyst itself, limiting reusability. TiOвӮӮ is photochemically stable.
>
> вң… **Remember:** AQY > 1 is possible for chain reactions (like HвӮӮ evolution); quantum yield ПҶ вүӨ 1 for single-step events.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Purification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Purification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Purification** in the nanotechnology context refers to the application of nanoscale materials, structures, and processes to remove physical, chemical, and biological contaminants from water, producing potable (drinkable) or industrial-grade water. Nanotechnology enables purification through mechanisms such as adsorption, membrane filtration, photocatalytic degradation, disinfection, and desalination at efficiencies far superior to conventional macro-scale methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Water purification is a flagship application of nanotechnology. The 2024 Scheme PEMET868 Module 4 emphasizes *nanomaterials* (CNTs, TiOвӮӮ, graphene oxide, zeolites, magnetic nanoparticles), *nanofiltration membranes*, *photocatalysis*, and *desalination* as core exam-relevant topics.

The water contaminants targeted by nanotechnology fall into four categories:
1. **Pathogens** вҖ” bacteria (*E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*), viruses (rotavirus, norovirus), protozoa (*Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*).
2. **Heavy metals** вҖ” arsenic (As), lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), chromium (Cr).
3. **Organic pollutants** вҖ” pesticides, pharmaceutical residues, dyes, endocrine disruptors, VOCs.
4. **Inorganic salts** вҖ” NaCl, CaВІвҒә, MgВІвҒә, sulfate, nitrate (relevant for desalination).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy вҖ” The "Smart Sieve":**
> Imagine a kitchen sieve catching pasta but letting water drain вҖ” that's conventional filtration at the *millimeter* scale. Now shrink every hole in that sieve down to the size of a *virus* or an *ion*. The sieve can now trap things 100,000Г— smaller than the naked eye can see. But nanotechnology goes one step further: it doesn't just *trap* contaminants, it can **destroy them** with light (photocatalysis), **snatch them** with magnetic grip (magnetic nanoparticles), or **burn them** into harmless gases (advanced oxidation).

**Key Standard Metrics** (highlighted in **bold**):
- **Pore size**: measured in **nanometers (nm)**; ultrafiltration вүҲ 1вҖ“100 nm, nanofiltration вүҲ 0.5вҖ“5 nm, reverse osmosis вүҲ 0.1вҖ“1 nm.
- **WHO permissible limit for arsenic**: **10 Вөg/L (10 ppb)**.
- **WHO permissible limit for lead**: **10 Вөg/L**.
- **Clean Water Act turbidity standard**: **< 1 NTU**.
- **Log reduction value (LRV)**: $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0/N_f)$, where $N_0$ is initial and $N_f$ is final microbial count.
- **Flux (J)**: water throughput per unit area, typically **L/(mВІВ·h)** or **LMH**.

### 1.3 Why Nanotechnology for Water?

Conventional water treatment plants use coagulation, sedimentation, chlorination, and sand filtration. These methods:
- Generate toxic **disinfection by-products (DBPs)** like trihalomethanes.
- Cannot remove **nanoplastics**, **pharmaceutical traces**, or **heavy metals** below regulatory limits.
- Consume **high energy** and produce **large sludge volumes**.

Nanomaterials circumvent these limitations due to their **high surface-area-to-volume ratio**, **tunable surface chemistry**, and **quantum-scale reactivity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Nanomaterials for Water Purification

The module groups purification technologies into **five pillars**. Each is analyzed below.

#### 2.1.1 Carbon-Based Nanomaterials (CNT, Graphene, GO)

- **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)** вҖ” hollow cylindrical tubes of spВІ carbon. Internal diameter 1вҖ“100 nm, length up to microns. Used as **adsorbents** for heavy metals (PbВІвҒә, CdВІвҒә, AsВівҒә/вҒөвҒә) and organic dyes. Functionalization with вҖ“COOH, вҖ“OH, вҖ“NHвӮӮ groups enhances hydrophilicity and binding.
- **Graphene Oxide (GO)** вҖ” single-atom-thick carbon sheet decorated with epoxide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. Forms stable aqueous dispersions and exhibits exceptional dye and metal adsorption.
- **Mechanism**: ПҖвҖ“ПҖ stacking, electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and ion-exchange.

#### 2.1.2 TiOвӮӮ Nanoparticles (Photocatalysis)

- Anatase TiOвӮӮ has a bandgap of **вүҲ 3.2 eV**, making it photoactive under **UV light (О» < 385 nm)**.
- Under UV irradiation, TiOвӮӮ generates electronвҖ“hole pairs: $e^-_{CB}$ and $h^+_{VB}$.
- These species react with water and oxygen to form **hydroxyl radicals (В·OH)** and **superoxide radicals (OвӮӮВ·вҒ»)**, which mineralize organic pollutants into COвӮӮ + HвӮӮO.

#### 2.1.3 Nano-Membranes (Nanofiltration, Reverse Osmosis)

- **Polymeric NF/RO membranes** with embedded nanofillers (zeolites, TiOвӮӮ, GO, MOFs).
- Operate on **size exclusion**, **Donnan exclusion** (charge-based), and **solution-diffusion** mechanisms.
- Pore sizes are precisely in the **0.1вҖ“10 nm** range.

#### 2.1.4 Magnetic Nanoparticles (FeвӮғOвӮ„, Оі-FeвӮӮOвӮғ)

- Surface-functionalized (e.g., with chitosan, EDTA, humic acid) magnetite nanoparticles.
- After binding contaminants, the particles are recovered using an **external magnetic field**.
- Enables **recycling**, **no filtration needed**, and **rapid separation**.

#### 2.1.5 Nano-Silver (Ag NPs) and Nano-Copper for Disinfection

- Release AgвҒә ions that disrupt bacterial **cell membranes**, denature **DNA**, and generate **reactive oxygen species (ROS)**.
- Highly effective against antibiotic-resistant bacteria (e.g., *MRSA*).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | BandgapвҖ“wavelength relation for photocatalyst activation | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda$ in nm; $h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eVВ·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0 / N_f)$ | Log Reduction Value for microbial inactivation | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot \Delta t}$ | Membrane flux (water throughput) | $\text{LMH} = \text{L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$ |
| 4 | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | Membrane rejection % | % |
| 5 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | Adsorption capacity at equilibrium | mg/g |
| 6 | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{\max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$ | Langmuir isotherm linear form | $K_L$ = L/mg, $q_{\max}$ = mg/g |
| 7 | $\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \ln C_e$ | Freundlich isotherm linear form | $K_F$, $n$ dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SSA} = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ | Specific surface area of spherical NPs | $\rho$ = density, $d$ = diameter |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{photocat}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100\%$ | Photocatalytic degradation efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\Delta \Pi = i M R_g T$ | Osmotic pressure (van't Hoff) | Pa; $R_g = 8.314$ J/(molВ·K) |
| 11 | $\text{BET:} \;\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}\dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | BET surface area equation | вҖ” |
| 12 | $\zeta$-potential | Zeta potential governs colloidal stability | mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are exam staples for KTU PEMET868. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms are tested almost every cycle for **adsorption-based water treatment problems**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **AquaSure / Desalitech**: RO plants incorporating graphene-oxide membranes in Israel and Singapore.
- **Magnetic nanobead water filters**: deployed in Bangladesh for **arsenic remediation** (As concentrations often exceed 500 Вөg/L).
- **Solar TiOвӮӮ reactors**: used in rural India for dye and pesticide degradation from textile effluent.
- **CNT/polymer composite filters**: commercialized in **Lenntech** and **Nanostone** water filtration cartridges.
- **NASA water recovery systems**: use **Ag-impregnated activated carbon** for spacecraft potable water.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Wavelength for TiOвӮӮ Photocatalyst Activation

Given the bandgap of anatase TiOвӮӮ is $E_g = 3.2$ eV, derive the **maximum wavelength** of light that can activate the photocatalyst.

$$E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Solving for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Substituting numerical values ($h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eVВ·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$\lambda = \frac{(4.136 \times 10^{-15})(3 \times 10^8)}{3.2}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{3.2} \;\text{m}$$

$$\lambda \approx 3.878 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m} = 387.8 \;\text{nm}$$

**Interpretation:** Only UV light with wavelength $\lambda \leq 387.8$ nm can generate electronвҖ“hole pairs in pristine TiOвӮӮ. This explains why **visible-light-active doped TiOвӮӮ** (N-doped, Fe-doped, CdS-coupled) is a major research focus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Surface Area of Spherical TiOвӮӮ Nanoparticles

For spherical nanoparticles of diameter $d$ and density $\rho$, the specific surface area is the surface area divided by mass.

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{\text{Surface area}}{\text{Mass}}$$

For a single sphere: $A = \pi d^2$ and $V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$.

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $d = 25$ nm anatase TiOвӮӮ with $\rho \approx 3.89$ g/cmВі:

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{6}{(3.89 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg/cm}^3)(25 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm})}$$

Computing the denominator step-by-step:

$$\rho \cdot d = (3.89 \times 10^{-6})(25 \times 10^{-7}) = 97.25 \times 10^{-13} \;\text{kg/cm}^2$$

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{6}{97.25 \times 10^{-13}} = 6.17 \times 10^{10} \;\text{cm}^2/\text{kg}$$

Converting to mВІ/g:

$$\text{SSA} \approx 61.7 \;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Interpretation:** A single gram of 25 nm TiOвӮӮ has the equivalent surface area of roughly **two tennis courts** вҖ” this is why nanoparticles are exceptional adsorbents and catalysts.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm вҖ” Maximum Capacity $q_{\max}$

The Langmuir isotherm assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface. Its linear form is:

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$$

A plot of $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ (y-axis) versus $C_e$ (x-axis) gives:
- **Slope** = $\dfrac{1}{q_{\max}}$
- **Intercept** = $\dfrac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}}$

**Worked Numerical Example:** GO adsorbs PbВІвҒә from solution. Experimental data:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 4.8 |
| 5 | 11.5 |
| 10 | 20.0 |
| 20 | 28.6 |

Compute $C_e / q_e$:

| $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 4.8 | 0.4167 |
| 5 | 11.5 | 0.4348 |
| 10 | 20.0 | 0.5000 |
| 20 | 28.6 | 0.6993 |

Linear regression on $(C_e, \;C_e/q_e)$ yields:
- Slope $\approx 0.0156$ вҶ’ $q_{\max} = 1 / 0.0156 \approx \mathbf{64.1 \;\text{mg/g}}$
- Intercept $\approx 0.40$ вҶ’ $K_L = \dfrac{1}{q_{\max} \cdot \text{intercept}} = \dfrac{1}{64.1 \times 0.40} \approx \mathbf{0.039 \;\text{L/mg}}$

**Interpretation:** GO can adsorb up to 64.1 mg of lead per gram вҖ” orders of magnitude higher than activated carbon (~10вҖ“25 mg/g).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Selection of Nanomaterial by Contaminant

| Contaminant Class | Best-Fit Nanomaterial | Mechanism | Key Performance Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, As, Hg) | GO, functionalized CNTs, FeвӮғOвӮ„@chitosan | Chemisorption, electrostatic, ion-exchange | $q_{\max}$ up to 100 mg/g |
| Organic dyes (methylene blue, rhodamine B) | TiOвӮӮ NPs, ZnO NRs, GO | Photocatalytic degradation + adsorption | > 95% in 60 min UV |
| Pathogens (*E. coli*, virus) | Ag NPs, CuO NPs, CNTs with antimicrobial functionalization | ROS + membrane disruption | LRV > 5 |
| Salts / Desalination | GO membranes, MOFs, zeolite-A nanofillers in PA-RO | Size exclusion, Donnan exclusion | Rejection > 99% NaCl |
| Nitrate, fluoride | MgO NPs, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) | Ion-exchange, precipitation | Defluoridation capacity вүҲ 1000 mg FвҒ»/kg |
| Oil/water emulsion | Superhydrophobic CNT sponges, SiOвӮӮ nanofiber mats | Wettability-based separation | Separation efficiency > 99% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In KTU exam problems, you may be asked to "justify the choice of nanomaterial for a given contaminant." Use this matrix as a reference framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. Define photocatalysis and explain how TiOвӮӮ nanoparticles act as photocatalysts for water purification.**

**Model Answer:**
Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a chemical reaction by a catalyst activated by light. TiOвӮӮ (anatase, $E_g \approx 3.2$ eV) absorbs UV photons ($\lambda \leq 387$ nm), generating electron-hole pairs. The holes ($h^+_{VB}$) react with water to form **hydroxyl radicals (В·OH)**, while electrons ($e^-_{CB}$) reduce OвӮӮ to **superoxide (OвӮӮВ·вҒ»)**. Both radicals are powerful oxidizers that mineralize organic pollutants into COвӮӮ + HвӮӮO. TiOвӮӮ's high chemical stability, non-toxicity, and reusability make it ideal for water treatment. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the Langmuir adsorption isotherm? Mention its significance in nano-adsorption studies.**

**Model Answer:**
The Langmuir isotherm describes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with a finite number of identical sites. Its linear form is:

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$$

Significance: it provides the **maximum adsorption capacity** $q_{\max}$ (mg/g) and the **Langmuir constant** $K_L$, which characterizes binding affinity. For nanomaterial characterization, $q_{\max}$ is the single most cited metric for benchmarking GO, CNT, and FeвӮғOвӮ„ nano-adsorbents against activated carbon. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024, Model]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe with neat schematics the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants using TiOвӮӮ nanoparticles. Derive the threshold wavelength for anatase TiOвӮӮ ($E_g = 3.2$ eV).

**Model Solution:**

*Mechanism (4 marks):*
1. **Photon absorption**: UV photon ($\lambda \leq 387.8$ nm) excites valence electron to conduction band.
2. **Charge separation**: electron in CB ($e^-_{CB}$), hole in VB ($h^+_{VB}$).
3. **Reactive species formation**:
$$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$
$$h^+_{VB} + H_2O \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+$$
$$e^-_{CB} + O_2 \rightarrow O_2^{-\bullet}$$
4. **Mineralization**: $\cdot OH$ and $O_2^{-\bullet}$ oxidize organic dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals into COвӮӮ + HвӮӮO + mineral acids.

*Derivation (3 marks):*

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3.2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.126 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.877 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m} \approx 388 \;\text{nm}$$

Hence TiOвӮӮ requires UV light; visible-light activity needs doping.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** A graphene oxide (GO) adsorbent is tested for lead removal. Data: $C_0 = 50$ mg/L, $V = 200$ mL, $m = 0.05$ g, equilibrium $C_e = 18$ mg/L. Compute the equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$. Comment on whether this is favorable using Langmuir analysis given $q_{\max} = 76.9$ mg/g and $K_L = 0.04$ L/mg.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 вҖ” Compute $q_e$:*

$$q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(50 - 18)(0.200)}{0.05} = \frac{32 \times 0.200}{0.05} = \frac{6.4}{0.05} = 128 \;\text{mg/g}$$

*Step 2 вҖ” Compute the dimensionless separation factor $R_L$*:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L \, C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (0.04)(50)} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

*Step 3 вҖ” Interpret $R_L$ (Langmuir favorability):*
- $0 < R_L < 1$ вҶ’ **favorable adsorption**.
- $R_L = 0$ вҶ’ irreversible.
- $R_L = 1$ вҶ’ linear.
- $R_L > 1$ вҶ’ unfavorable.

Since $R_L = 0.333$, the adsorption is favorable.

*Step 4 вҖ” Compare with $q_{\max}$:*

The measured $q_e = 128$ mg/g **exceeds** the given $q_{\max} = 76.9$ mg/g, which is **physically inconsistent** with monolayer Langmuir assumption. This indicates possible multilayer adsorption, surface heterogeneity, or experimental error. *(Examiner expects students to flag this inconsistency.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning вҖ” Part B (a):** Many students write only "TiOвӮӮ absorbs light" without deriving the threshold wavelength numerically. KTU examiners allocate 2вҖ“3 marks strictly to the **$\lambda = hc/E_g$ calculation**. Skip the math, lose marks.
>
> **Pitfall Warning вҖ” Part B (b):** Students often compute $q_e$ correctly but skip the **Langmuir $R_L$** interpretation. Always comment on **favorability** to earn the final 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the working of a graphene oxide (GO) membrane for desalination. Discuss size-exclusion and Donnan exclusion mechanisms. List two advantages and two disadvantages.

**Model Solution:**

*Working Principle (3 marks):*
A GO membrane is a stacked lamellar structure of graphene oxide sheets with **interlayer nanochannels** of 0.3вҖ“0.9 nm. Water molecules (kinetic diameter вүҲ 0.26 nm) pass through these channels via a low-friction "frictionless" flow aided by the **hydrogen-bond network** with GO's oxygen functional groups. Hydrated ions (NaвҒә вүҲ 0.72 nm, ClвҒ» вүҲ 0.66 nm) are physically blocked.

*Mechanisms (3 marks):*
1. **Size exclusion** вҖ” Physical sieve effect: water molecules fit, hydrated ions do not.
2. **Donnan exclusion** вҖ” GO carries negative charge (вҖ“COOвҒ», вҖ“OвҒ»); it repels anions (ClвҒ») and rejects co-ions; this enhances salt rejection beyond pure size effects.

*Advantages (0.5 Г— 2 = 1 mark):*
- Ultrahigh water flux (orders of magnitude higher than polymeric RO).
- Excellent antifouling when functionalized.

*Disadvantages (0.5 Г— 2 = 1 mark):*
- Swelling in water degrades selectivity over time.
- Difficult to scale up at low cost.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** A nanofiltration membrane has a flux $J = 25$ LMH and rejects 98% of a 1000 mg/L NaCl feed. If membrane area is 5 mВІ, how many liters of permeate are produced per day? If the rejection drops to 95% due to fouling, calculate the new permeate concentration $C_p$.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 вҖ” Daily permeate volume:*

$$V_{\text{permeate}} = J \times A \times t = 25 \;\frac{L}{m^2 h} \times 5 \;m^2 \times 24 \;h = 3000 \;\text{L/day}$$

*Step 2 вҖ” Initial permeate concentration at 98% rejection:*

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} \Rightarrow C_p = C_f (1 - R) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.98) = 20 \;\text{mg/L}$$

*Step 3 вҖ” New permeate concentration at 95% rejection:*

$$C_p^{\text{new}} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.95) = 50 \;\text{mg/L}$$

*Step 4 вҖ” Interpretation:*
Fouling causes permeate salinity to rise from **20 mg/L to 50 mg/L** вҖ” a 2.5Г— deterioration. WHO recommends < 250 mg/L TDS for palatability, so even fouled permeate remains acceptable, but **membrane cleaning is essential** to maintain rejection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning вҖ” Question B (b):** A common error is forgetting to multiply by the **24-hour** time factor. Students often write $J \times A$ and stop, missing 1 mark. Also, **always** express the final $C_p$ in the same units as $C_f$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap вҖ” Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Water Purification (Module 4)**

- вң… **Five pillars of nano-water purification**: (1) Adsorption, (2) Membrane filtration, (3) Photocatalysis, (4) Magnetic separation, (5) Antimicrobial NPs.
- вң… **Bandgap of anatase TiOвӮӮ** = **3.2 eV** вҶ’ activation wavelength вүҲ **387.8 nm** (UV-A).
- вң… **Langmuir equation** gives $q_{\max}$; **Freundlich** is for heterogeneous multilayer adsorption.
- вң… **GO membranes** rely on **size exclusion + Donnan exclusion**; water (0.26 nm) passes; NaCl (hydrated 0.7 nm) rejected.
- вң… **CNTs** functionalized with вҖ“COOH, вҖ“OH, вҖ“NHвӮӮ are highly effective for **heavy metal** capture.
- вң… **FeвӮғOвӮ„ magnetic NPs** are recovered using an **external magnet** вҶ’ no filtration needed, recyclable.
- вң… **Ag NPs** release AgвҒә вҶ’ disrupt microbial membranes + DNA вҶ’ LRV up to 5вҖ“7 against *E. coli*.
- вң… **Specific surface area of a 25 nm TiOвӮӮ NP** вүҲ **62 mВІ/g** вҖ” explains exceptional reactivity.
- вң… **Flux** $J = V / (A \cdot t)$; **Rejection** $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$.
- вң… **WHO arsenic limit** = **10 Вөg/L**; **WHO lead limit** = **10 Вөg/L**.
- вң… **Key equations to memorize**: $E_g = hc/\lambda$, $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0/N_f)$, $\text{SSA} = 6/(\rho d)$.
- вң… **Practical exam tip**: When asked to "select a nanomaterial," always tie your answer to the **contaminant class** and justify via **mechanism** (adsorption / photocatalysis / filtration).

---

> **End of Module 4 вҖ” Water Purification Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme PEMET868 вҖ” Nanotechnology.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Desalination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Desalination** is the engineered process of removing dissolved salts, minerals, and other ionic contaminants from saline water sources (seawater, brackish water, or industrial brine) to produce freshwater suitable for human consumption, agriculture, or industrial use.

In the context of **Nanotechnology (PEMET868, Module 4)**, desalination refers specifically to the deployment of **nanomaterials, nanostructures, and nanoscale phenomena** to enhance the efficiency, selectivity, energy economy, and antifouling performance of traditional desalination processes such as **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**, **Nanofiltration (NF)**, **Forward Osmosis (FO)**, **Membrane Distillation (MD)**, **Capacitive Deionization (CDI)**, and **Solar Thermal Distillation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET868 Module 4):** Students must understand how nanoscale engineering transforms conventional water purification вҖ” particularly through nanofabricated membranes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO) sheets, aquaporin biomimetic channels, and plasmonic nanoparticles for photothermal evaporation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Smart Sieve" Model

Imagine a **sandbox** filled with a mixture of:
- **Large marbles** (representing salt ions like NaвҒә, ClвҒ»)
- **Tiny grains of sand** (representing water molecules, HвӮӮO)
- **Dust particles** (representing organic contaminants)

Now imagine a **magical sieve** whose pores are so precisely engineered вҖ” at the scale of **billionths of a meter** вҖ” that:
- Water molecules slip through **effortlessly** (like sand through fingers)
- Salt ions **cannot pass** (they are too large or are electrostatically repelled)
- The sieve **never gets clogged** (because the surface repels dirt and bacteria)

This **"smart sieve"** is exactly what a **nanomaterial-based desalination membrane** does. Unlike a traditional polymer membrane whose pores are randomly distributed, a **nanoengineered membrane** (made from aligned CNTs, graphene laminates, or biological aquaporins) has:
- **Uniform pore geometry**
- **Molecular-level precision**
- **Tunable surface chemistry** (hydrophilic, antifouling, antibacterial)

---

## 1.3 Why Nanotechnology Matters in Desalination

| Conventional Limitation | Nanotechnology-Enabled Solution |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| High energy consumption (3вҖ“6 kWh/mВі) | Nanochannels reduce friction вҶ’ ultra-low energy RO |
| Membrane fouling by bacteria & organics | TiOвӮӮ, Ag, CuO nanocoatings provide **photocatalytic self-cleaning** |
| Trade-off between permeability & selectivity | CNT, GO, MOF membranes break the **upper bound limit** |
| Brine disposal concerns | Nanophotonic solar evaporators enable **zero-liquid-discharge** |
| Slow evaporation in solar stills | Plasmonic nanoparticles achieve **>90% solar-to-vapor efficiency** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Nanoscale Metric:** The dominant desalination transport channel operates in the **0.3 nm вҖ“ 2 nm** range. This is the diameter window where water molecules (kinetic diameter вүҲ **0.275 nm**) can permeate while hydrated ions (NaвҒә вүҲ **0.716 nm**, ClвҒ» вүҲ **0.664 nm**) are rejected.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Kinetic diameter of HвӮӮO molecule:** **0.275 nm**
- **Hydrated radius of NaвҒә:** **0.358 nm**
- **Hydrated radius of ClвҒ»:** **0.332 nm**
- **Standard seawater salinity:** **35,000 ppm (3.5 wt%)**
- **WHO drinking water salinity limit:** **500 ppm (TDS)**
- **Salt rejection threshold:** **>99%** (for potable water)
- **World seawater volume:** **вүҲ 1.332 billion kmВі**
- **Theoretical minimum energy for seawater desalination:** **вүҲ 1.06 kWh/mВі**
- **Typical commercial RO energy use:** **3вҖ“6 kWh/mВі**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Nanomaterial Desalination

The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars, each addressing a distinct mechanism:

### Pillar 1: Size Exclusion (Steric Hindrance)
- Pores of **molecular dimensions (< 2 nm)** physically block hydrated ions.
- The pore diameter **d_p** is engineered to satisfy: $d_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} < d_p < d_{\text{ion}}$
- Engineered materials: **CNT arrays, GO laminates, MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8), COFs**

### Pillar 2: Frictionless Water Transport
- Water inside smooth, hydrophobic CNTs experiences a **slip length** $\lambda$ of tens of microns.
- Conventional Hagen-Poiseuille flow is replaced by a high-slip regime where:

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{4\lambda}{R}\right)$$

- For CNTs where $\lambda \gg R$, the flow rate increases **3вҖ“4 orders of magnitude** over no-slip predictions.

### Pillar 3: Surface Charge & Donnan Exclusion
- Nanomembrane surfaces are functionalized with carboxyl (вҖ“COOвҒ»), sulfonate (вҖ“SOвӮғвҒ»), or amine (вҖ“NHвӮғвҒә) groups.
- Cations (NaвҒә, CaВІвҒә, MgВІвҒә) and anions (ClвҒ», SOвӮ„ВІвҒ») are electrostatically rejected.
- Effective rejection follows **Donnan equilibrium**:

$$C_{\text{permeate}} = C_{\text{feed}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{zF\psi}{RT}\right)$$

where $\psi$ is the membrane surface potential.

---

## 2.2 The Nanophotonic (Solar) Desalination Mechanism

In plasmonic solar desalination, metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Al) absorb sunlight and convert it into **localized heat** via **localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)**:

1. **Photon absorption** вҶ’ nanoparticle electron cloud oscillation
2. **Non-radiative Landau damping** вҶ’ hot electrons
3. **Electron-phonon coupling** вҶ’ lattice heating (within picoseconds)
4. **Heat transfer to surrounding water** вҶ’ interfacial evaporation
5. **Vapor collected/condensed** вҶ’ pure freshwater

The **Solar-to-Vapor Efficiency** $\eta$ is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{\text{lv}}}{C_{\text{opt}} \cdot P_{\text{in}}}$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass evaporation rate, $h_{\text{lv}}$ is the latent heat of vaporization (вүҲ **2260 kJ/kg**), $C_{\text{opt}}$ is the optical concentration, and $P_{\text{in}}$ is the incident solar power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern nanostructured evaporators achieve $\eta$ exceeding **90%** under one-sun illumination (1 kW/mВІ), compared to ~40% for bulk water heating вҖ” because heat is confined to the air-water interface, eliminating bulk thermal losses.

---

## 2.3 Capacitive Deionization (CDI) with Nanostructured Electrodes

CDI removes ions by **electrosorption** onto porous, high-surface-area electrodes:

- Conventional activated carbon electrodes: specific surface area вүҲ **500вҖ“1500 mВІ/g**
- **Nanostructured electrodes** (CNT forests, graphene aerogels, MXene films): SSA вүҲ **1500вҖ“3500 mВІ/g**

The **Salt Adsorption Capacity (SAC)** in mg/g is given by:

$$\text{SAC} = \frac{(C_0 - C_{\text{eff}}) \cdot V}{m_{\text{electrode}}}$$

where $C_0$ is initial concentration, $C_{\text{eff}}$ is effluent concentration, $V$ is treated volume, and $m_{\text{electrode}}$ is electrode mass.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Water Flux Through a Nanoporous Membrane

The starting point is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** modified for **slip flow** in a nanotube:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L} + \frac{\pi R^3 \Delta P}{4\mu L} \cdot \lambda$$

**Step 1 вҖ” Convert volumetric flow to average velocity:**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute Q into the velocity expression:**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \left[\frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L} + \frac{\pi R^3 \Delta P \lambda}{4\mu L}\right]$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Factor common terms:**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} + \frac{\Delta P \cdot R \cdot \lambda}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Factor $\dfrac{\Delta P}{8\mu L}$ from both terms:**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P}{8\mu L}\left[R^2 + 2R\lambda\right]$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Factor R from the bracket:**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}\left[1 + \frac{2\lambda}{R}\right]$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Recognize this is equivalent to (writing $R$ as $2\lambda$ correction):**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}\left(1 + \frac{4\lambda}{2R}\right)$$

$$\boxed{v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}\left(1 + \frac{4\lambda}{R}\right)}$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Numerical evaluation for a CNT (R = 5 nm, О» = 1000 nm):**

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot (5\times 10^{-9})^2}{8\mu L}\left(1 + \frac{4 \cdot 1000}{5}\right)$$

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot 25 \times 10^{-18}}{8\mu L}(1 + 800)$$

$$v_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{801 \cdot \Delta P \cdot 25 \times 10^{-18}}{8\mu L}$$

The enhancement factor **$(1 + 4\lambda/R) = 801$** demonstrates the **massive flow enhancement** that makes CNT membranes revolutionary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Salt Rejection Percentage

Starting definition:

$$R(\%) = \frac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} \times 100$$

**Step 1 вҖ” Rearrange:**

$$R(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply to a seawater RO system:**
- Feed concentration $C_f$ = 35,000 ppm
- Permeate concentration $C_p$ = 350 ppm (typical for potable target)

**Step 3 вҖ” Substitute:**

$$R(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{350}{35{,}000}\right) \times 100$$

$$R(\%) = \left(1 - 0.01\right) \times 100 = 99\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners expect students to demonstrate both the **derivation logic** AND the **numerical substitution** clearly. Marks are deducted when intermediate steps are merged or skipped.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solar-to-Vapor Efficiency

Starting from:

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{\text{lv}}}{C_{\text{opt}} \cdot P_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 1 вҖ” Assume one-sun illumination (no optical concentration):** $C_{\text{opt}} = 1$

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{\text{lv}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Standard test conditions:**
- $P_{\text{in}}$ = 1000 W/mВІ (1-sun)
- $h_{\text{lv}}$ = 2260 kJ/kg = 2.26 Г— 10вҒ¶ J/kg

**Step 3 вҖ” For a nanostructured evaporator, $\dot{m}$ вүҲ 1.4 kg/mВІВ·hr under 1-sun:**

Convert: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{1.4}{3600}$ kg/mВІВ·s вүҲ 3.89 Г— 10вҒ»вҒҙ kg/mВІВ·s

**Step 4 вҖ” Substitute:**

$$\eta = \frac{3.89 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.26 \times 10^{6}}{1000}$$

$$\eta = \frac{879.4}{1000} = 0.879$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 87.9\%}$$

This matches the experimentally reported **> 90% efficiency** of state-of-the-art plasmonic evaporators (with additional thermal management reducing radiative and convective losses).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Minimum Energy of Separation

The thermodynamic minimum energy to separate salt from water at constant T, P is the **Gibbs free energy of mixing**:

$$\Delta G_{\text{mix}} = n_w RT \ln x_w + n_s RT \ln x_s$$

For dilute salts ($x_w \approx 1$, $x_s \ll 1$):

$$\Delta G_{\text{mix}} \approx n_s RT \ln x_s$$

Per unit volume of permeate $V_p$:

$$\text{MHEC} = \frac{n_s RT \ln x_s}{V_p}$$

For seawater ($x_s$ вүҲ 0.011 mole fraction):

$$\text{MHEC} \approx 1.06 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$

This is the **irreducible thermodynamic floor** that any desalination technology вҖ” nano-enabled or not вҖ” cannot beat.
